o Average daily rate (ADR)
o Revenue per available room (RevPAR)
o Gross operating profit per square metre
o Customer satisfaction
o Carbon footprint
Notes
Variance reporting
The original budget should be flexed to reflect the actual level of activity and to ensure that variance calculations are not
distorted by a different activity level to what was expected. Separating the variable and fixed elements of costs would also allow
for better identification of which costs vary with activity.
More balanced measures
More measures are needed for effective control. There are no customer satisfaction measures. If you are not undertaking
customer surveys, then these should be considered. If already undertaken, they should be reported in management accounts.
Corporate social responsibility and carbon footprint are external drivers for responsible practices. Ethical consumerism is driving
the need for responsible business practises. Measures reflecting the reduced levels of food waste and energy usage could be
used to show that sustainability is important for the business.
Key performance indicators (KPIs) needed to support decision making for a hotel may include:
Occupancy rates: Paid rooms occupied/Rooms available
It is important to demonstrate the efficiency and operational performance of the planned hotel development. The existence
of occupancy rates which are less than the hotel capacity means that there are lost selling opportunities and reduced hotel
revenue. It is important that the occupancy rate is high enough to ensure the variable expenses are covered and there is a
contribution to fixed costs.
ADR: Total room revenue/Paid rooms occupied
ADR measures the average price paid per available room. It is a measure of financial performance and profitability. Profitability
is likely to be strongly correlated to room revenues and maximising these revenues is going to be a top priority. This is a rate
which could be used for comparison against past performance and to the competition.
RevPAR: Total room and related revenues/total rooms available
This measure incorporates both room rates and occupancy. It provides a snapshot of how well the business is filling its rooms,
as well as how much it is able to charge. Rising RevPAR is an indication that either occupancy is improving, or room rates are
rising or a combination of the two. This is only measuring one revenue stream and will need to be considered.
Gross operating profit per square metre
RevPAR focuses on room revenue, but where there is more revenue coming from other areas, it would be beneficial to analyse
that more widely. It will help with comparisons of hotels of different sizes and non-room revenue. It will indicate how well the
hotel is using the space available to it to drive its economic performance. It can be used to measure the profitability of individual
areas to ensure they are achieving the best value from the space they use. Is the Spa area or the bar area generating a better
return and if logistics allow should there be a change in activities to make better use of the space?
Customer satisfaction
Non-financial measures are important for hotels and can help raise their profile, particularly in the age of social media.
Customer satisfaction surveys and feedback forms can be used to monitor praise and repeat business, and also complaints in
order to effect improvements. If feedback from surveys is acted upon, it will show to the customer that you value their business,
attract loyalty from them and build profitability through repeat custom and referrals.
Carbon footprint
Reduction of the carbon footprint of the hotel is a key external driver of sustainability and socially responsible business
practices. Customers and other stakeholders are increasingly asking for carbon footprint improvements. The development of
eco-labels is helping customers make their choice and it will be important to provide evidence of proof of compliance. Hotels
are big consumers of energy. A reduction in the kilograms of carbon used each guest night would be one example of a good
measure in this age of increased ethical consumerism.

by going into the hotel business (up to a maximum of 6 marks).
– Explanation of ideal form of diversification, i.e. to engage in activities which behave in exactly opposite ways, adverse
consequences in one area can be offset by participation in a sector which is performing well.
– Both the restaurant and the hotel are in the same sector and will move in the same direction with fluctuations in the
economy.
– Risk may be partially reduced by spreading the activities into different areas of the hospitality business, however, to
generate substantial reductions in unsystematic or specific risk, Nehby would need to move into markets outside of the

December 2023 1
1. Introduction
NCTech is a well-known cloud service company operating in Farland, a developed country
which is the home to many technological companies. NCTech was founded by three software
engineers.
NCTech has grown dramatically during its ﬁrst 10 years of operation. The three founding owners
remain fully committed to the business and currently hold the positions of chief executive ofﬁcer,
commercial director, and operations director on the NCTech board.
2. Cloud service industry information
Industry overview
Cloud service companies offer data storage facilities, with
many also providing a wide range of computing services over
the internet (‘the cloud’) to user organisations (clients).
Competitive pricing has made cloud services a ﬁnancially
viable option for client organisations of all sizes.
The cloud services industry in Farland has been established for
more than a decade and continues to grow to meet the needs
and demands of the market, particularly amongst small and
medium sized enterprises, and individual professionals.
Key cloud service statistics 20X2
Key statistics 20X2 20X2
Global Farland
Percentage of client data stored in the cloud 52% 38%
Total annual revenue generated by all cloud services
providers
Top 10 cloud services companies’ share of the market
$155 billion
74%
$8·3 billion
85%
Cloud service models
Cloud service companies can provide three service models to clients.
Infrastructure as a service (IaaS)
IaaS is a basic service which provides networks, servers and data storage facilities directly over
the internet. With IaaS, clients lease server capacity for data processing and storage.
Clients use their own in-house expertise to install and run their software applications on their own
operating system.
Platform as a service (PaaS)
PaaS provides clients with all the services provided by IaaS, and in addition it offers
communication software which enables access to the cloud service provider’s operating systems.
PaaS allows clients to run their own software applications on the provider’s operating systems.
Software as a service (SaaS)
SaaS provides clients with all the services provided by PaaS and it is also where particular
software applications are hosted centrally by the cloud service provider for use by clients. SaaS

满足市场的需求和要求，特别是满足了中小型企业和个

Commented [7]: 云服务模式
云服务公司可以为客户提供三种服务模式。
Commented [8]: 基础设施即服务（IaaS）
IaaS是一种基本服务，直接通过互联网提供网络、服务
器和数据存储设施。通过IaaS，客户租用服务器容量进
行数据处理和存储。
客户使用他们自己的内部专业人士在自己的操作系统上
安装和运行他们的软件应用程序。
Commented [9]: 平台即服务（PaaS）
PaaS向客户提供IaaS提供的所有服务，此外，它还提供
通信软件，使客户能够访问云服务提供商的操作系统。
PaaS允许客户在云服务提供商的操作系统上运行自己的
软件应用程序。
allows data to be accessed and processed by the client on the cloud server as if it were installed
on a client’s own computer system.
With SaaS, the cloud service provider effectively handles all components of the service, so the
client does not need its own specialist IT hardware and software. As SaaS is a premium service,
it is the most expensive.
The range and extent of services offered under each model determines the fees chargeable to
clients.
The table below summarises the different cloud services provided under each service model.

Cloud service delivery
There are three ways in which the three cloud service models can be delivered:
The public cloud offers the required technological infrastructure and services over the cloud
to any organisation or individual on a ‘pay as you use’ basis. The public cloud does not target
particular clients or client groups, and it is hosted from the cloud service provider’s facilities.
The private cloud provides computing infrastructure for the exclusive use of speciﬁc business
clients. It is best suited for large businesses which demand considerable, and more complex,
computer processing capability. The private cloud can either be located in a client’s facility or
hosted at a designated cloud services third party commercial site. Private cloud services are
charged at a premium price.
The hybrid cloud is a combination of both public and private cloud models. More important
aspects of the client’s operations can be hosted on a private cloud facility, with more general
business functions run on the public cloud. This provides clients with greater operational ﬂexibility
and although it is more expensive than the public cloud, it is signiﬁcantly cheaper than the
private cloud.
Beneﬁts of cloud services to clients
There are a number of reasons why cloud services are an attractive option to clients.
1.
2.
3.
4.
5.
Cloud service provision is easily scalable both up and down, reducing costly and signiﬁcant
changes to a client’s technology infrastructure.
Clients can have instant access to cloud services systems, avoiding lengthy system
development and implementation.
Cloud service companies have enhanced security measures which reduce threats to
client’s data and system integrity.
Data can be accessed and processed at any time over the cloud from any remote location
or using any devices, including mobile devices.
The cloud enables clients to share data easily and it supports collaborative working over

云服务有如下三个交付方式：

。客户运营更重要的方面可以托管在私有云设施上，而

Commented [16]: 云服务给客户带来的好处
diverse locations.
6.
7.
Cloud services are supported and maintained by the cloud service provider, reducing the
need for clients to employ expensive IT specialists to maintain their in-house systems.
Clients do not require expensive space to accommodate hardware, as the technology is
managed and accommodated by the cloud service provider.
Cloud service industry challenges and developments in Farland
There are a number of signiﬁcant challenges which the Farland cloud service industry is currently
facing:
1. The Farland cloud service sector is growing very rapidly and is anticipated to double in size
over the next ﬁve years. This means that both the nature and extent of competition within the
industry is likely to change considerably over this period, with clients’ demands becoming
more challenging to satisfy.
2.
3.
More global competition is emerging so the industry may become dominated by a small
number of large, well-established IT companies. This could have the effect of reducing the
inﬂuence of smaller Farland cloud service companies and consolidating the industr y.
Clients are highly dependent on a reliable high-speed internet connection for cloud
services to work ef fectively. Although this is currently not an issue in Farland as there are a
number of different internet providers, it is critical that the Farland internet and broadband
infrastructure keeps pace with demand.
4. There is a constant risk from hacking, data loss, and distributed denial of service from
unscrupulous external sources. A distributed denial of service attack involves excessive
activity on the system from an unidentiﬁed source which signiﬁcantly slows down the
system and reduces its operational performance.
5.
6.
The regulatory requirements associated with cloud services are evolving and may turn out
to be more challenging to comply with. The regulatory demands may become more costly
and onerous, particularly for smaller cloud service companies.
There is constant demand for highly skilled and technically knowledgeable employees to
work within the cloud service industr y. However, the availability of suitably qualiﬁed staff for
this rapidly growing sector means that they are in short supply, and very costly to employ.
Cloud service innovation
The following areas of technological development have been identiﬁed as being vital to the
future success of all cloud service businesses which provide the SaaS service model within the
Farland cloud service industr y.
Business intelligence [BI] BI systems collate, store and process data to provide clients
with meaningful information in support of decision-making. If BI
were offered on the cloud, it would satisfy the growing demands
from clients for access to BI systems combined with the other
beneﬁts of cloud applications.
Quantum computing Quantum computing performs complex data processing more
efﬁciently than a normal computer. If quantum computing is
offered on the cloud, it could dramatically increase both the
processing capability and performance of clients. This could
prove to be a unique selling point for a cloud service provider.

越来越多的全球竞争正在出现，因此该行业可能会被少
数大型、成熟的IT公司所主导。这可能会降低规模较小
的Farland云服务公司的影响力，并整合行业。
客户高度依赖可靠的高速互联网连接，云服务才能有效
地工作。虽然目前这不是一个问题，因为有许多不同的
互联网提供商，但至关重要的是，Farland的互联网和宽
带基础设施要跟上需求的步伐。
黑客攻击、数据丢失和来自无良外部来源的分布式拒绝
服务的风险始终存在。分布式拒绝服务攻击涉及来自不
明来源的系统过度活动，这会大大降低系统速度并降低
其操作性能。
与云服务相关的监管要求正在不断发展，可能会变得更
具挑战性。监管要求可能会变得更加昂贵和繁重，尤其
是对规模较小的云服务公司而言。
云服务行业对高技能和技术知识渊博的员工的需求不断
增加。然而，这一迅速发展的领域缺乏合格的适当工作
人员，这意味着他们供不应求，而且雇用成本非常高。Commented [20]: 云服务创新
Commented [21]: 以下技术发展领域已被确定为对
Farland云服务行业内提供SaaS服务模式的所有云服务业
务的未来成功至关重要。
Commented [22]: 商业智能[BI]：BI系统对数据进行整理
、存储和处理，为客户提供有意义的信息以支持决策。
如果在云上提供BI，它将满足客户对访问BI系统的不断
增长的需求，并结合云应用程序的其他好处。
Commented [23]: 量子计算：量子计算比普通计算机更
有效地执行复杂的数据处理。如果在云端提供量子计算
，它可以极大地提高客户端的处理能力和性能。这可能
被证明是云服务提供商的一个独特卖点。
Artiﬁcial intelligence (AI)
Machine learning (ML)
AI is technology which simulates human intelligence and is
programmed to ‘think and act’ like human users. If available
over the cloud, it would enable clients to access and harness its
advanced capabilities within their business operations at a lower
cost.
Machine learning is a more sophisticated form of AI which
allows applications to more accurately forecast results and
outcomes without being explicitly programmed to do so. Having
this facility on the cloud could be a game changer for many
clients who would not otherwise be able to afford such complex
technology. ML includes a diverse range of technologies
including voice recognition and virtual assistants.

Security breaches

4.

given period.

taxation, as a percentage of the revenue generated.

employed
3.
4.

Security breaches The ﬁnancial impact of cloud security breaches in any given
period. This comprises compensation paid to affected clients,
and lost revenue from forecast future business.

3. NCTech
Background
NCTech is a well-known cloud service company operating in Farland. NCTech was established
and incorporated 10 years ago by three software engineers. They recognised that the rapid
growth in demand for cloud services presented them with a great opportunity to use their
combined knowledge and skills to set up a business in this ﬁeld.
NCTech operates as a public cloud services company so that it can target a wide client base of
small to medium sized companies (SMEs) throughout Farland. NCTech provides comprehensive
data storage facilities, a range of servers for the storage, retrieval and sending of data, and the
required networking resources necessary to provide the IaaS service model. NCTech has never
offered the PaaS or SaaS service models.
Ownership
The initial shareholder investment was supported by venture capital, which was repaid when
NCTech listed on the Farland stock exchange ﬁve years ago. Each of the three original
shareholders still has a 10% stake in NCTech. The remaining shares are held by a number of
institutional investors, such as pension funds and unit trusts, but no single shareholder holds
more than 10% equity.
The three founding owners remain fully committed to the business and currently hold the positions
of chief executive ofﬁcer, commercial director and operations director on the NCTech board. The
current board membership has not changed in the last ﬁve years.
Shares in NCTech have performed better than other similar technology companies on the Farland
stock exchange over the last ﬁve years. However, in the last ﬁnancial year there has been an
unforeseen and signiﬁcant fall in gross proﬁt, which has affected the share price and unsettled
some shareholders.
The absence of any dominant shareholders means that the board is generally free to run the
business in the best interests of the whole company, without any direct or signiﬁcant interference.
Vision, mission, and corporate values
The NCTech corporate vision is to be one of the leading cloud
service providers in Farland
The mission of NCTech is to become a world-class cloud service company by:
–
–
–
–
–
Constantly achieving operational excellence.
Differentiating ourselves by exceeding the expectations of our clients.
Developing and maintaining a strong NCTech brand.
Developing sustainable relationships with all key business stakeholders.
Maintaining a proﬁtable business model to attract further investment.

STRATEGIC
BUSINESS LEADER
PRE-SEEN INFORMATION
Applicable for the December 2023 Exam
December 2023 1
Contents
1. Introduction  .........................................................................................................................2
2. Cloud service industry information ....................................................................................2
Industry overview .................................................................................................................2
Key cloud service statistics 20X2 ........................................................................................2
Cloud service models  .........................................................................................................2
Cloud service delivery  ........................................................................................................3
Benefits of cloud services to clients  ...................................................................................3
Cloud service industry challenges and developments in Farland ......................................4
Cloud service innovation .....................................................................................................4
Operational and financial key performance indicators [KPIs] .............................................5
3. NCTech overview ................................................................................................................6
Background  .........................................................................................................................6
Ownership  ............................................................................................................................6
Vision, mission and corporate values ..................................................................................6
NCTech board structure ......................................................................................................7
Risk register .........................................................................................................................7
Current strategic position ....................................................................................................8
Performance dashboard ......................................................................................................9
December 2023 2
1. Introduction
NCTech is a well-known cloud service company operating in Farland, a developed country
which is the home to many technological companies. NCTech was founded by three software
engineers.
NCTech has grown dramatically during its fi  rst 10 years of operation. The three founding owners
remain fully committed to the business and currently hold the positions of chief executive offi  cer ,
commercial director , and operations director on the NCTech board.
2. Cloud service industry information
Industry overview
Cloud service companies offer data storage facilities, with
many also providing a wide range of computing services over
the internet (‘the cloud’) to user organisations (clients).
Competitive pricing has made cloud services a fi  nancially
viable option for client organisations of all sizes.
The cloud services industry in Farland has been established for
more than a decade and continues to grow to meet the needs
and demands of the market, particularly amongst small and
medium sized enterprises, and individual professionals.
Key cloud service statistics 20X2
Key statistics 20X2
Global
20X2
Farland
Percentage of client data stored in the cloud 52% 38%
Total annual revenue generated by all cloud services
providers $155 billion $8·3 billion
Top 10 cloud services companies’ share of the market 74% 85%
Cloud service models
Cloud service companies can provide three service models to clients.
Infrastructure as a service (IaaS)
IaaS is a basic service which provides networks, servers and data storage facilities directly over
the internet. With IaaS, clients lease server capacity for data processing and storage.
Clients use their own in-house expertise to install and run their software applications on their own
operating system.
Platform as a service (PaaS)
PaaS provides clients with all the services provided by IaaS, and in addition it offers
communication software which enables access to the cloud service provider’s operating systems.
PaaS allows clients to run their own software applications on the provider’s operating systems.
Software as a service (SaaS)
SaaS provides clients with all the services provided by PaaS and it is also where particular
software applications are hosted centrally by the cloud service provider for use by clients. SaaS
December 2023 3
allows data to be accessed and processed by the client on the cloud server as if it were installed
on a client’s own computer system.
With SaaS, the cloud service provider effectively handles all components of the service, so the
client does not need its own specialist IT hardware and software. As SaaS is a premium service,
it is the most expensive.
The range and extent of services offered under each model determines the fees chargeable to
clients.
The table below summarises the different cloud services provided under each service model.
IaaS PaaS SaaS
Provision of data servers   
Data storage facilities   
Networking resources   
Communications software  
Operating systems  
Data software applications 
Cloud service delivery
There are three ways in which the three cloud service models can be delivered:
The public cloud  offers the required technological infrastructure and services over the cloud
to any organisation or individual on a ‘pay as you use’ basis. The public cloud does not target
particular clients or client groups, and it is hosted from the cloud service provider’s facilities.
The private cloud provides computing infrastructure for the exclusive use of specific business
clients. It is best suited for large businesses which demand considerable, and more complex,
computer processing capability . The private cloud can either be located in a client’s facility or
hosted at a designated cloud services third party commercial site. Private cloud services are
charged at a premium price.
The hybrid cloud  is a combination of both public and private cloud models. More important
aspects of the client’s operations can be hosted on a private cloud facility , with more general
business functions run on the public cloud. This provides clients with greater operational flexibility
and although it is more expensive than the public cloud, it is significantly cheaper than the
private cloud.
Benefits of cloud services to clients
There are a number of reasons why cloud services are an attractive option to clients.
1. Cloud service provision is easily scalable both up and down, reducing costly and significant
changes to a client’s technology infrastructure.
2. Clients can have instant access to cloud services systems, avoiding lengthy system
development and implementation.
3. Cloud service companies have enhanced security measures which reduce threats to
client’s data and system integrity .
4. Data can be accessed and processed at any time over the cloud from any remote location
or using any devices, including mobile devices.
5. The cloud enables clients to share data easily and it supports collaborative working over
December 2023 4
diverse locations.
6. Cloud services are supported and maintained by the cloud service provider , reducing the
need for clients to employ expensive IT specialists to maintain their in-house systems.
7. Clients do not require expensive space to accommodate hardware, as the technology is
managed and accommodated by the cloud service provider .
Cloud service industry challenges and developments in Farland
There are a number of significant challenges which the Farland cloud service industry is currently
facing:
1. The Farland cloud service sector is growing very rapidly and is anticipated to double in size
over the next five years. This means that both the nature and extent of competition within the
industry is likely to change considerably over this period, with clients’ demands becoming
more challenging to satisfy .
2. More global competition is emerging so the industry may become dominated by a small
number of large, well-established IT companies. This could have the effect of reducing the
influence of smaller Farland cloud service companies and consolidating the industry .
3. Clients are highly dependent on a reliable high-speed internet connection for cloud
services to work effectively . Although this is currently not an issue in Farland as there are a
number of different internet providers, it is critical that the Farland internet and broadband
infrastructure keeps pace with demand.
4. There is a constant risk from hacking, data loss, and distributed denial of service from
unscrupulous external sources. A distributed denial of service attack involves excessive
activity on the system from an unidentified source which significantly slows down the
system and reduces its operational performance.
5. The regulatory requirements associated with cloud services are evolving and may turn out
to be more challenging to comply with. The regulatory demands may become more costly
and onerous, particularly for smaller cloud service companies.
6. There is constant demand for highly skilled and technically knowledgeable employees to
work within the cloud service industry . However , the availability of suitably qualified staff for
this rapidly growing sector means that they are in short supply , and very costly to employ .
Cloud service innovation
The following areas of technological development have been identified as being vital to the
future success of all cloud service businesses which provide the SaaS service model within the
Farland cloud service industry .
Business intelligence [BI] BI systems collate, store and process data to provide clients
with meaningful information in support of decision-making. If BI
were offered on the cloud, it would satisfy the growing demands
from clients for access to BI systems combined with the other
benefits of cloud applications.
Quantum computing Quantum computing performs complex data processing more
efficiently than a normal computer . If quantum computing is
offered on the cloud, it could dramatically increase both the
processing capability and performance of clients. This could
prove to be a unique selling point for a cloud service provider .
December 2023 5
Artificial intelligence (AI) AI is technology which simulates human intelligence and is
programmed to ‘think and act’ like human users. If available
over the cloud, it would enable clients to access and harness its
advanced capabilities within their business operations at a lower
cost.
Machine learning (ML) Machine learning is a more sophisticated form of AI which
allows applications to more accurately forecast results and
outcomes without being explicitly programmed to do so. Having
this facility on the cloud could be a game changer for many
clients who would not otherwise be able to afford such complex
technology . ML includes a diverse range of technologies
including voice recognition and virtual assistants.
Operational and financial key performance indicators [KPIs]
Operational KPIs
Service availability 1. The amount of time the service is actually available as
a proportion of the total amount of time that the service is
scheduled to be available to the client. The target is always
100%.
Capacity utilisation 2. A measure of the proportion of the available cloud services
which have been allocated for use by clients.
Note: The allocated service may not be fully utilised at all
times, but it remains available for clients if  they choose, so it
cannot be reallocated.
Service reliability 3. The number and duration of reported service failures in a
given period.
Security breaches 4. The number of security breaches identified in a given period.
Financial KPIs
Average revenue/client 1. The average amount of revenue generated from an individual
client, over a given period.
Operating profit margin 2. The profit recorded before the payment of interest on debt and
taxation, as a percentage of the revenue generated.
Return on capital
employed
3. The operating profit derived from the capital employed by the
business over a given period, expressed as a percentage.
Security breaches 4. The financial impact of cloud security breaches in any given
period. This comprises compensation paid to affected clients,
and lost revenue from forecast future business.
December 2023 6
3. NCTech
Background
NCTech is a well-known cloud service company operating in Farland. NCTech was established
and incorporated 10 years ago by three software engineers. They recognised that the rapid
growth in demand for cloud services presented them with a great opportunity to use their
combined knowledge and skills to set up a business in this field.
NCTech operates as a public cloud services company so that it can target a wide client base of
small to medium sized companies (SMEs) throughout Farland. NCTech provides comprehensive
data storage facilities, a range of servers for the storage, retrieval and sending of data, and the
required networking resources necessary to provide the IaaS service model. NCTech has never
offered the PaaS or SaaS service models.
Ownership
The initial shareholder investment was supported by venture capital, which was repaid when
NCTech listed on the Farland stock exchange five years ago. Each of the three original
shareholders still has a 10% stake in NCTech. The remaining shares are held by a number of
institutional investors, such as pension funds and unit trusts, but no single shareholder holds
more than 10% equity .
The three founding owners remain fully committed to the business and currently hold the positions
of chief executive officer , commercial director and operations director on the NCTech board. The
current board membership has not changed in the last five years.
Shares in NCTech have performed better than other similar technology companies on the Farland
stock exchange over the last five years. However , in the last financial year there has been an
unforeseen and significant fall in gross profit, which has affected the share price and unsettled
some shareholders.
The absence of any dominant shareholders means that the board is generally free to run the
business in the best interests of the whole company , without any direct or significant interference.
Vision, mission, and corporate values
The NCTech corporate vision is to be one of the leading cloud
service providers in Farland
The mission of NCTech is to become a world-class cloud service company by:
– Constantly achieving operational excellence.
– Differentiating ourselves by exceeding the expectations of our clients.
– Developing and maintaining a strong NCTech brand.
– Developing sustainable relationships with all key business stakeholders.
– Maintaining a profitable business model to attract further investment.
December 2023 7
NCTech core values
Energy: Grasp opportunities and make things happen.
Excellence: Strive to do things better than anyone else in our
industry.
Excitement: Foster challenge, trust, and respect to and from
colleagues through teamwork.
Ethics: Promote and show integrity and openness in all areas of
business.
NCTech board structure
Non-executive
chair
Non-executive
directors
x 5
Finance
director
Commercial
director
Marketing
director
Human
resources
director
Operations
director
Chief
executive
officer
There are four sub-committees:
– Audit committee, comprising three non-executive directors all of whom have a strong
background in finance.
– Nominations committee, which is run by the non-executive chair and comprises all
non-executive directors.
– Remuneration committee, comprising three non-executive directors with the human
resources director providing the members with professional guidance.
– Risk committee, comprising two non-executive directors and the commercial director and
operations director .
NCTech is fully compliant with the Farland corporate governance code.
Risk register
NCTech maintains a register of principal risks.
The risk committee determines the nature and extent of the risks NCTech is willing to accept
to deliver success. The risk committee is also responsible for maintaining and monitoring the
systems of internal control and risk management.
Risk management activities are co-ordinated by a team led by a risk manager who reports to
both the operations director and to the chair of the risk committee.
December 2023 8
NCTech’s principal business risks include:
1. Competitive threats
– New entrants capturing existing NCTech market share.
– Technological obsolescence.
– Market pressures affecting pricing.
2. Staffing concerns
– Loss of key personnel.
– Bypassing internal procedures and controls.
– Regulatory breaches.
– Unethical behaviour and/or decision-making affecting business operations.
3. Technology issues
– Lack of internet availability; key to accessing cloud services.
– Service availability on a ‘24 hours a day , 7 days a week’ basis.
– Virus contamination of client data, operating systems, and applications.
4. Data security
– Unauthorised access to client data.
– Data breach or loss, probably from a hacker .
– Distributed denial of service attack which involves excessive activity on the system
from an unidentified source which significantly slows down the system and reduces
its operational performance.
5. Regulatory changes
– New regulations relating to the cloud service industry introduced by Farland authorities.
Current strategic position
NCTech periodically appraises its position and status in the Farland cloud service industry . The
recent findings are summarised below:
Strengths
– A good reputation with the core client base.
– Strong relationship with clients.
– The provision of secure and reliable services, competitively priced.
– A capable and competent workforce.
Weaknesses
– The IaaS service model offered to clients has never changed, so restricts growth potential.
– Several large, overseas cloud service companies have recently entered the Farland market.
– Deteriorating retention of key members of staff.
– Worsening financial performance over the last financial year .
– High dependency on internet service providers, to enable clients to access the NCTech
IaaS system.
December 2023 9
Performance dashboard

The chair of the risk committee has undertaken an investigation into a recent data breach
at NCTech and emailed his preliminary findings to the chief executive officer (CEO). The
CEO is concerned that the resultant bad publicity could significantly harm NCTech’s
relationship with its client base and may have a detrimental effect on its business
performance going forward.
The CEO has provided you with a copy of the email and asked for your assistance.
(a) Write a memo to the CEO which evaluates the information from the chair of the
risk committee, the possible impact on NCTech’s reputation and recommends actions
that NCTech should take.
(14 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for commercial acumen by demonstrating awareness
of how the data breach could damage NCTech’s reputation.
(4 marks)
The recent security breach has forced the board to consider enhancements to the current IT
security arrangements at NCTech.
You have been provided with the relevant extracts from NCTech’s current IT security
manual and have been asked to evaluate it and recommend any further measures that will
help to strengthen these existing controls and reduce the risk of a future data breach.
(b) Draft a briefing paper to the board that evaluates the four security layers in
NCTech's IT security manual and recommends any further security controls that should
be put in place to minimise the risk of a future data breach.
(14 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for analysis by considering the current IT security
manual and any additional security controls that could minimise the risk of a future data
breach.
(4 marks)

company listed on the Farland stock exchange five years ago, however two board members

He has asked you to advise him on issues related to board diversity.

Professional skills marks are available for scepticism by probing into the issues relating to

Suggested Solution
Task 1
Requirement 1(a)
To: Chief People Officer
From: Senior Business Analyst
Date: September 20X3
Briefing Notes
The impact of leadership style on Corjetz’s culture
Background – problems of original culture and leadership style
Prior to the appointment of our current CEO, Corjetz’s culture had been driven by the
entrepreneurial spirit of the original founders. The stories of ‘entrepreneurial spirit’ are likely
to have been prominent in Corjetz’s cultural web in its first few years. However, the
organisational structure had become hierarchical and control systems were based on
punishment rather than reward. This clearly had an impact on staff morale, as staff turnover
was high 10 years ago, which was impacting adversely on customer service levels.
Leadership was more autocratic with most of the major decisions being made by the senior
managers. This lack of delegation is likely to have been another factor increasing staff
turnover.  Therefore, up to the appointment of the current CEO, the culture was largely
power based, with authority and strict control systems being dominant.
Appointment of Magda Fisk – leadership style change
Since her appointment, Magda Fisk has set about changing the culture of Corjetz by
implementing a more democratic leadership style, which has been achieved through
encouraging greater participation. Magda has focused more on Corjetz’s staff, recognising
their importance in achieving high levels of customer service. Magda could be described as
a ‘transformational’ leader, one who sees their role as a leader to motivate and inspire
others. By doing this she has moved Corjetz’s culture into a new direction, away from one
focused on senior management control and power to one where delegation, innovation and
trust are central.
Important aspects of the CEO leadership style
• Prior to Magda’s appointment, the structure of the organisation was hierarchical and
there seemed to be little connection between the senior leaders and staff. However,
Magda encourages a more consultative and participatory approach to leadership.
Leaders must not only tell their ideas, they need to also sell them, which she has
encouraged through the monthly ‘meet the board’ online intranet sessions. Bringing
board members closer to the staff by encouraging dialogue helps to create a
connection between staff at all levels and helps staff to understand and appreciate
the work of the directors.
• Customer service is clearly an important factor for Corjetz, considering the highly
competitive nature of the industry. Before her appointment, this lacked innovation
and was routine, which presumably was a contributing factor to the low levels of
customer satisfaction at this time. By encouraging innovation and participation, staff
are more likely to recognise the importance of dynamic customer service and are
encouraged to put forward ideas to improve this area of the business.
• Changing the control systems within Corjetz’s cultural web has also been an
important part of Magda’s approach. Rewarding staff through staff development and
training has had a positive impact on staff retention, which seems to have positively
impacted on customer satisfaction levels.
• Setting up staff representative committees encourages a culture of innovation and
knowledge sharing. More importantly, this involvement and participation in decision
making has empowered and engaged staff which has been a key driver in improving
levels of customer service.
• Even by obtaining staff input into re-designing the cabin staff uniforms, this itself
creates a sense of team spirit and staff engagement in one of the key symbols of the
organisation’s culture. Getting staff to buy into a key element of its culture is an
important way to engage and retain staff.
• Staff retention levels would suggest staff who are better motivated and want to
remain employed by Corjetz. In turn, this is likely to have positively impacted on
levels of customer satisfaction.
Summary
A transformational leadership style has played a key role in changing the behaviours and
motivation of Corjetz’s staff. This leadership approach taken by our CEO has created a
greater level of participation and delegation which has increased staff motivation and
retention. This transformational style has also changed elements of Corjetz’s cultural web
which has resulted in a greater focus on customer service, through more motivated and
engaged staff.
Requirement 1(b)
(i) Assess the key roles of Corjetz’s NEDs
Challenge strategy
The chair and CEO should use the NEDs to provide general counsel and a different
perspective on Corjetz’s strategic direction. As ‘outsiders’, our NEDs will have a wider view
of external factors affecting the company and the wider airline business environment than
our executive directors. The key role of our NEDs in strategy formation is to provide a
creative and informed contribution and to act as constructive critics in reviewing the
objectives and plans devised by our executive board.  In addition, NEDs can help to connect
the business and board with networks of potentially useful people and organisations in the
wider airline and aviation industry.
Monitor performance
Our NEDs should take responsibility for monitoring the performance of the executive board,
especially the progress being made towards achieving company strategy and overall
objectives. They have a key role in appointing, and removing executive directors, and in
succession planning. NEDs should hold the executive board to account for the decisions
they have made, and the results achieved. This is particularly important to our shareholders,
who will expect our NEDs to rigorously monitor and challenge performance on their behalf.
For example, the latest financial results for 20X3 show a decline in performance and the
shareholders would expect Corjetz’s NEDs to question the executive board as to why
performance has declined, and the strategies being implemented to address any
weaknesses.
Risk scrutiny and management
NEDs should satisfy themselves on the integrity and accuracy of financial information. They
must also ensure that they are satisfied that our financial controls and systems of risk
management are correctly in place and that they are robust and defensible.
This task is carried out by Corjetz’s Audit Committee, which is a committee normally made
up mostly of independent NEDs which provides an overall check on the work of executive
management in the areas of financial management and reporting and that suitable systems
of risk management and control are in place. Corjetz also has a robust ERM framework in
place, which is overseen and monitored by the NEDs.
Remuneration and appointment of executive directors
Our NEDs also have responsibility for the appointment of new executive directors and for
deciding the remuneration of executive directors and senior managers. NEDs play a key role
Key roles of Corjetz’s NEDs
• Challenge strategy
• Monitor performance
• Risk scrutiny and management
• Remuneration and appointment of directors
in succession planning for key directorial positions in the organisation.  NEDs will also play a
role in any contractual negotiations and arrangements for senior positions in the business
and will be involved in any disciplinary actions needed at board and senior management
level.
Slide 2
(ii) Assess the importance to Corjetz of having a diverse board
Wider pool of experience and knowledge
Promoting diversity opens up a much wider pool of talented and experienced board
members. This is critically important at a time of increasing competition in the global airline
business environment, where a diverse board with varied experience and perspectives of
aviation and the wider general business community will mean Corjetz is better placed to
understand not only the airline environment but also the wider business environment and the
stakeholders within it.
More effective decision making
A diverse board helps to reduce the potential for ‘groupthink’ which can occur when board
members are either over familiar with each other’s ways of thinking or because there is too
much similarity in their opinions, styles or beliefs. Diversity should assist in approaching
issues from a much wider perspective and set of opinions and therefore there is more
chance that challenge will take place. This should, in turn, result in greater debate and
consideration of strategic matters which should result in better quality decisions.
Improve reputation
Having a diverse board membership sends out a positive message to our external
stakeholders that we are a sound corporate citizen and that we value the opinions of a
diverse range of sources. This can help to position us as a socially responsible organisation
that is non-discriminatory and by reflecting the diversity of the society which we serve, we
will achieve a better social contact with our external environment, and we are likely to attract
a more diverse set of employees.
Improve investor relations
Our shareholders are likely to be highly supportive of having a diverse board membership.
The improvement in the quality of strategic decisions and the reputational benefits achieved
from having a diverse board are likely to enhance shareholder confidence and attract more
shareholders to invest in the business.
Importance of having a diverse board
• Wider pool of experience and knowledge
• More effective decision making
• Improve reputation
• Improve investor relations
Suggested solution
Task 2
Requirement 2(a)
To: Chief finance officer
From: Senior business analyst
Date: September 20X3
REPORT
Assessment of investment in Artificial Intelligence (AI) flight simulation technology
Introduction
The following report will consider the suitability, acceptability and feasibility of a proposal by
the chief commercial officer to invest in AI flight simulation technology for Corjetz’s pilots.
The report will consider a range of internal and external environmental impacts of this
investment and make a final recommendation based on this analysis.
Suitability
When considering the suitability of the investment, we need to assess whether it is a realistic
response to our strategic position and whether it fits with Corjetz’s environment and strategy.
Firstly, it is estimated that the new training technology would significantly reduce training
costs for Corjetz. Currently we spend over $140 million training our pilots each year, which is
a critical investment. As an organisation which follows a low-cost strategy, the proposed
investment would seem to support this strategic position. However, we must consider
whether the investment would assist us in achieving our values and maintaining our strategic
position.
Lowering training costs would impact positively on our overall profitability, which is a key
metric for our business. Also, as an organisation we strive to be a data-driven organisation
which embraces the latest technological developments in the industry and therefore, this
investment would certainly support this.
However, I would challenge the suggestion that this investment would contribute towards
strengthening Corjetz’s competitive advantage. To gain competitive advantage we must be
achieving something that sets us apart from our competitors in the eyes of our customers.
To do this, the AI driven pilot training must provide our pilots with superior or enhanced
training that impacts on the quality of the delivery of our service. It is not evident from the
investment proposal that this is the case. All that we currently know is that the training can
be provided more quickly and at less cost. There is no suggestion that the pilots are better
trained or have superior skills because of this training. Therefore, I would question the
assertion that investment in this technology would provide competitive advantage at this
point in time.
Acceptability
We also need to assess the risks, returns and stakeholder expectations from this proposed
investment.
Risks
The risks involved in this technology are high, as it is yet to be fully tested and implemented
in a commercial airline setting. The investment proposal suggests that it is currently
successfully operating in a military setting, but the technology is still not capable of
replicating all the potential scenarios needed when flying a commercial aircraft. As this has
not been fully tested in a commercial setting there is a risk that the anticipated cost and time
savings are in fact not realistic, and costs may be higher than presented.
Until all flight scenarios have been fully tested and certified by the aviation regulators, the
potential risks of this technology for our customers, pilots and cabin crew are significant
which is likely to make this investment unacceptable at the moment. A further risk is the lack
of available specialist instructors in this area. We may invest in this technology and then find
that we do not have the number of available specialist instructors to operate the technology
effectively.
Returns
However, the returns are likely to be positive for Corjetz in the long term as the current costs
of training are high and seemingly can be reduced quite significantly by this investment. The
proposal suggests that initial training costs for a new pilot could be reduced by over 40% and
on-going training costs for all pilots could be reduced by over 55%. These savings would
have a positive impact on the profit of the business, but the ROCE may not be positively
impacted to the same extent due to the high levels of investment needed. The initial cost of
$500 million is a significant expenditure and we would need to carefully consider how we
would finance this and the impact that this would have on our shareholders.
Stakeholder expectations
We also need to consider the expectations of a stakeholders of this proposed investment.
Our pilots may embrace this technology as it will mean significantly less time in initial training
and in updating their skills.  AI based training may even attract new pilots to join Corjetz, as
we would be seen as being at the forefront of commercial pilot training. Our pilots are a key
asset to our business, and it is critical that we get their buy-in to this technological
investment. However, there is also an argument that pilots may be reluctant to accept this
technology if they believe that it is not capable of replicating the scenarios that they will
encounter in real life flying scenarios. We prioritise a commitment to safety in our values,
and until full testing is carried out, then this commitment cannot be guaranteed. Pilots are
likely to require evidence of its full capabilities, and therefore the suggestion that pilots will
welcome this investment may be questioned.
Our shareholders will need to be convinced of the long- term benefits and returns of this
investment. Primarily they will be concerned with the financial returns of the proposal. This is
a significant investment of $500 million. Shareholders may be concerned about the potential
reputational impact of this investment. Although it could have positive reputational benefits
from being a leader in AI driven pilot training, should the AI training be of lower standard or
not meet required regulations resulting in a serious incident then this would have disastrous
consequences for the business. So, we would need to consult with our shareholders
extensively.
We also must consider other stakeholders other than pilots and shareholders. Our
customers are a key stakeholder, and they may not be confident flying with pilots trained
only on AI technology. Although most customers will not be aware of how our pilots are
trained, as this is a cutting-edge technology and we are proposing to be the first to
implement it, then it is likely to receive some press coverage. Therefore, we must be able to
alleviate any customer concerns should they arise.
Our industry regulators are likely to take a keen interest in this investment and also
governments throughout Hundria are likely to enforce extremely tight regulations on the
adoption of AI in pilot training. From the proposal this is likely to be a slow process and no
doubt will add additional costs to the implementation of this technology.
Feasibility
The initial cost of $500 million is significant. I do not currently have access to information
about sources of finance for this investment, so it is not possible to comment on the financial
feasibility in depth. However, it is anticipated that the necessary finances would likely be
available if the investment was accepted. However, consideration of the impact of the
funding method on shareholders would need to be undertaken.
There is a concern that there is currently a lack of specialist instructors needed to operate
this technology effectively. Hiring and training such instructors will take time and additional
costs which need to be factored into the decision.
Although this technology has been implemented successfully in a military setting, this does
not necessarily mean it will be equally successful in a commercial airline setting. Although
the technology itself would seem to be suitable in terms of being in line with the strategic
position of Corjetz as a cost leader, it still requires further feasibility testing in the context of
our overall business environment.
Recommendation
Although there are many positive attributes of the AI investment proposal, currently there are
too many unknowns to invest in this technology. Significant research and further
investigation need to be undertaken into its full scope and the full costs and savings
potentials. Also, involvement of key stakeholders would be advisable, particularly the pilots.
It is clearly an exciting development and one which we need to maintain awareness of and
involvement in, but it is advised that Corjetz gather further information. As this is not
considered to be an area of competitive advantage, there is no real need to be the first
mover in implementing AI pilot training technology.
Requirement 2(b)
To: Chief finance officer <CFO@Corjetz.com>
From: Senior business analyst <SBA@Corjetz.com>
Date: September 20X3
Subject: Principal risk review
Please find below the information you requested on the dynamic nature and potential
consequences of the principal risks for your response to the risk manager.
(i) The dynamic nature of principal risks
It is arguable that all risks identified in our enterprise risk management process are ‘dynamic’
in nature. Risks are unlikely to be fixed, particularly in a fast-moving industry such as
aviation. It is critical therefore that Corjetz is proactive in its risk management response. As
detailed in your email, our principal risks continue to evolve over time and therefore we must
develop and evolve our risk management activities as our risk exposure changes.
The risks outlined are particularly dynamic for the following reasons:
• Technologies are increasingly important in the airline industry and are continually and
rapidly changing, which brings with it a wide range of threats. These include
increasing risks of external malicious attacks and threats to business continuity. The
sophistication and speed of development of external attacks is increasing and
therefore a risk which must be considered highly dynamic.
• Additionally, customer expectations of airline businesses are constantly increasing in
terms of service delivery and safety. Customers are increasingly exposed to external
influences, driven by wide range and easy access to the internet, social media and
competitor advertising. This enhanced awareness of the industry and what it offers,
brings significant benefits but also increases risks to the business, as customer
needs and expectations are constantly evolving and increasing.
• The competitive environment is also increasingly dynamic. The airline industry is
highly competitive (evidenced by 30 low-cost airlines presently operating within
Hundria) and as these businesses fight for customers, this puts huge pressure on us
to keep customers satisfied and to operate profitably to keep our shareholders
satisfied. More competitors and increasing competitor activity is also putting huge
pressures on airports which is impacting our costs and ability to operate effectively.
• Probably one of the most dynamic risks we face is climate change and the resultant
reaction to this from customers, governments and regulators.  The impact of climate
change on weather patterns in recent years has seen significant disruption to flights.
Importantly we are likely to see a significantly greater impact on our business from
legislation and external pressure groups in the coming years. The airline industry has
been subject to severe scrutiny and criticism for its impact on climate change and
CO2 emissions and this seems only to be intensifying.
(ii) Potential consequences of these risks for Corjetz
1. External attack on or failure of critical technology
• An external attack or failure of critical technology would likely result in system
unavailability for customers and/or staff. Staff would not be able to access key
operational and financial systems which would likely bring all operations to a halt.
Similarly, attacks on or failure of customer-based systems could result in lost
bookings or an inability for customers to print out vital travel/ flight documentation
• External attacks or systems failures would present potentially severe operational
disruption to the business, resulting in damage to our reputation and loss of customer
confidence.
• In the most severe case, an attack on in-flight control systems or air traffic control
systems could have fatal consequences for customers and crew.
• Malicious external attacks are likely to result in the loss of critical business and/or
personal customer data. There is likely to be an adverse reputational impact if
customer data is lost or stolen. Legal and compensation costs could also be
significant.
2. Significant safety/ security event
• A significant safety or security breach could be catastrophic for Corjetz. Significant
injury or in the worst-case scenario, loss of life due to an air accident or a security
incident would have major consequences for the business.
• There would inevitably be sustained adverse media coverage and we would likely
lose customer confidence.
• This would lead to a reduction in future revenue which would have an adverse impact
on our share price, and we would also likely lose investor confidence.
• It is likely that we would face some form of fine or penalty which is likely to be
extremely costly.
• Inevitably we would face severe operational disruption in the aftermath of any safety
or security incident which would impact on our revenues and profit.
• A significant security breach could result in overall business failure.
3. Climate change and eco policies and regulations
• Climate change, causing more adverse weather conditions is likely to result in
adverse customer experience. This will be due to disruption and delays and
potentially could result in injury to customers
• There is also likely to be operational disruption due to airspace and runway closures.
• In extreme cases, we may incur aircraft damage in extreme storm or difficult landing
conditions. This could be extremely costly.
• In terms of eco-policies, we may face significant increases in aviation taxes/ levies
from local governments in countries where we fly.
• Legislation to cut aircraft global CO2 emissions may constrain growth and capacity,
resulting in pressure on margins as we seek to find alternative ways to deliver our
operations in an environmentally friendly way.
• Political changes and priorities in different countries will make it challenging to keep
up to date with the increasing global awareness of climate change. If we fail to keep
up, then we may lose competitive advantage or face a customer backlash.
Suggested solution
Task 3
Requirement 3(a)
To: Chief finance officer
From: Senior business analyst
Date: September 2023
Performance evaluation y/e June 30th 20X3
Introduction
The following report will assess the provisional performance of Corjetz in the latest financial
year. This will include an assessment of a range of both financial and non-financial
performance measures.
(Appendix 1)

Headline Financial Results ($ millions) 20X3 20X2

Summary Statistics 20X3 20X2

Passengers (millions) 86.5 79.7   8.5%  F
Number of routes operated 978 912   7.2%  F
Revenue per passenger $ 66.4 66.6   -0.3%  A
Total cost per passenger $ 62.0 60.0   3.3%  A
KPIs 20X3 20X2

104,328
96,280   8.4%  F
Revenue Per Available Seat Kilometer $
(RASK) 0.06 0.06  -  -

Return On Capital Employed % 9.3 13.0   -3.7%   A
Customer Satisfaction % 77.0 78.0  -1.0%  A
Punctuality % 76.0 79.0  -3.0%  A
Staff turnover % 5.2 5.9  -0.7%  F
CO2 Per passenger (grams) 82.0 88.0   -6.8%   F
Evaluation of 20X3 performance
Headline financial results:
Revenues have increased by 8.3% which is positive considering the highly competitive
environment in which we operate.  If we look deeper, we can see that this has been driven
by increased passenger numbers, which is also a positive indicator for the business.
Increasing passenger numbers each year is an important aspect of our business and a key
determinant of success. However, revenue per passenger has slightly decreased (0.3%),
meaning our seat prices are not moving up in line with costs.  This is further evidenced by
the cost per passenger which has risen by 3.3%. This is likely due to the increased
competitive challenges we face and that our customers are very price sensitive. Our
strategic position and a key determinant of our competitive success is a low cost/ low price
operator and increasing prices is likely to result in a loss of customers and the loss of our
competitive position. We have increased passenger numbers which is highly positive. But
increasingly, competitors are likely to be forcing us to discount our prices further to maintain
our position.
Total costs have increased at a greater rate than our revenues in the year. Costs excluding
fuel increased by nearly 10%, these are likely to be partly driven by higher staff costs from
flying more routes (7.2%), which inevitably will result in higher costs for the business in
operating these flights both in the air and on the ground. We know that airport charges are
rising, as airports are increasingly facing capacity challenges and this will continue to impact
on our business in the future, as growth is likely to be limited by the airport capacity available
to us.
Fuel costs also increased by nearly 20% in the year. We have operated more routes in
20X3, which will inevitably impact on fuel costs. However, we will need to investigate this
further as fuel is a considerable proportion of Corjetz’s costs and therefore any further
increase in fuel prices could impact significantly on profit. We must re-evaluate our fuel
purchasing policy and our currency hedging activities to protect against fuel price
fluctuations in the major countries where we fly and purchase fuel.
As a result of the above, we have seen profit before tax fall by 26.4%. This is a significant
reduction, and we will need to take a wider view of these results in the context of the whole
industry. The airline industry is driven by many external factors, particularly current political
agendas, economic cycles and consumer trends and we would need to look at the wider
picture of the whole industry before drawing conclusions about our headline performance.
However, shareholders are likely to be concerned with falling profits. A starting point will be
to ensure that we thoroughly review our pricing strategy and our cost management and
control activities throughout the whole business and review the profitability of each route that
we offer.
KPIs and Summary statistics
Although we flew more seats in 20X3, not all of these were revenue generating, as
passenger numbers did not increase at the same rate. Therefore, a significant number of the
seats flown were empty (8 million in 20X3 (94.5-86.5) compared to 6 million in 20X2 (85.7-
79.7)). This has resulted in an adverse change in passenger load factor of 1.5%. This could
be due to new routes not yet attracting customers at the same level as our established and
most popular destinations. Therefore, we may need to evaluate new routes in more detail to
establish their load factor and consider how we can improve passenger load through
increased advertising or alternative pricing strategies.
Return on Capital Employed has fallen from 13% to 9.3%.  This has been in part due to our
reduced profit for the year and may also have been caused by greater investment in capital
employed. I do not have any information on this, but it could be assumed that if we are
delivering 7.2% more routes then we will have needed to employ more aircraft to achieve
this.
A concerning feature of performance in 20X3 is the reduction in customer satisfaction levels.
Although not a significant percentage drop, it is still concerning, as customer performance hit
an all-time high last year and therefore it is disappointing that we have not been able to
sustain this or even improve on it. This reduction in customer satisfaction could be driven by
the reduction in punctuality which dropped 3%. This could be driven by the increasingly
congested airports and the increasing demand for airspace, meaning that delays are
becoming more frequent. However, customer satisfaction is a complex issue and may be
caused by several factors, including in-flight service levels, accessibility and usability of our
online booking systems and airport experience. Therefore, we must investigate all these
areas to establish where we need to improve to increase our customer satisfaction levels.
We appear to be performing well in terms of CO2 emissions, with emissions per passenger
reducing 6.8%. This is likely to be increasingly driven by the fuel-efficient airplanes that we
invest in and is a positive indicator of our commitment to our environmental and
sustainability commitment.
Another positive area of performance is staff turnover, which has reduced from 5.9% to 5.2%
of staff in the year. Again, this supports our continued commitment to improve employee
engagement through training and development as part of our environmental and
sustainability commitment.
Summary
Performance in 20X3, both financial and non-financial has been disappointing overall.
Although we have flown more passengers, this has not transferred into more profit for the
business, as costs have increased at a greater rate than revenues. This will inevitably
disappoint our shareholders who will be concerned that costs are not being managed
effectively.
Although Corjetz flew more passengers across more routes, these are clearly not yet
converting into profit. However, it may be too early to assess the viability of these additional
routes, as it may take more time to become established and attract more passengers and
therefore increase load factor.
It is of concern that our customer satisfaction has reduced, and this is an area we must
prioritise. Our customers are highly price sensitive and retaining loyalty and repeat business
is key to our business model.
There have been some positives in performance, notably that passenger numbers did
increase which means that we are attracting increasing numbers of customers to our flights
and destinations. Also, our CO2 emissions have significantly reduced per passenger which is
a positive indicator of our work towards reducing our impact on the environment.
Requirement 3(b)
To: Chief finance officer
From: Senior business analyst
Date: September 20X3
Briefing Paper
Environmental and sustainability audit report content
Overview
An environmental and sustainability audit report should provide Corjetz’s key stakeholders
with an assurance of the environmental and sustainability management policies, processes
and activities that are undertaken by the organisation. Such a report should primarily focus
on the four key principles outlined in Corjetz’s environmental and sustainability dashboard.
However, it should also consider the overall commitment of leadership to environmental and
sustainability activities and a review of the organisation’s commitment to stakeholder
engagement regarding these priorities.
Key content
1. Review of overall leadership/ commitment to the sustainability
The audit report should begin with an assessment of Corjetz’s leadership with regard to its
environmental and sustainability priorities. The audit report will assess the policies and
procedures put in place by senior management to maintain the overall focus of sustainability
practices throughout the business. The audit report should evaluate the overall level of
commitment by assessing cultural focus on sustainability.
The report should contain a review of evidence that there is a commitment to promoting a
culture that is  aware of environmental and sustainability issues. The dashboard itself
provides evidence of leadership focus and commitment on sustainability and the
environment.
2. Review of stakeholder engagement
The audit report should identify and assess evidence of wider stakeholder involvement in
environmental and sustainability activities. It should report on any relevant committees or
meetings relating to the key priorities of environmental and sustainability issues (such as any
reports and meeting held by the Safety Committee). It will also report on policies and
interactions with external stakeholders such as aircraft suppliers and external contractors
regarding their actions and responsibilities for maintaining environmental and sustainability
standards. The report will contain evidence of any actions taken and implemented from
stakeholder engagement activities (such as training and development for staff in
environmental awareness).
3. Audit assessment and findings of key priorities
Climate responsibility
The report should consider the overall attitudes of leadership and management to climate
responsibility. The audit report should focus on the controls implemented to manage and
minimise carbon emissions, energy usage, waste management and noise reduction. The
report would include an assessment of the objectives and targets set by the business in
these key activities and the achievement of these.
Safety
The audit report should include an assessment of the key health and safety management
procedures and controls put in place. This will include an evaluation and assessment of the
adequacy of training policies implemented and assess any potential link with training and
levels of Health and Safety incidents in the year. Security protocols with be assessed against
industry standards and reported. Health and safety controls should be of particular interest to
auditors, and they should report on key incidents and potential reasons and
recommendations for improvement.
Customer and supplier relationships
The audit report should contain an assessment of the systems and controls in place to
manage customer relationships. Evidence should be presented of systems to deal with
customer complaints and an evaluation of the effectiveness of these systems. Interviews
with customers and customer feedback response analysis may provide some explanation of
this evidence.
Supplier selection and management policies need to be assessed and reported.
Responsibilities and procedures for supplier selection and monitoring should be reported, as
should results of any KPI’s set for key suppliers. Evidence of ethical supplier selection and
management policy statements should be reviewed and reported. Evidence of actions taken
to prevent bribery or fraud should be included.
Employee relationships
The audit report should contain evidence that the employee management process, from
selection through to termination is operating effectively and objectively. In particular, the
report should focus on the level and adequacy of training for specific job roles.  The outcome
of employee engagement surveys should also be assessed and evaluated and any
recommended actions from this feedback has been actioned.
Summary
An annual environmental and sustainability audit report should focus on reassuring
stakeholders that Corjetz’s leadership is taking positive actions and is maintaining a positive
culture that promotes achievement and development of its environmental and sustainability
priorities. The focus should be on the key priorities but should also reassure stakeholders
that the overall culture of the business is driving towards sustainability.
