stem_zh:
Dec 24 Mock exam
Background
This case study relates to three tasks.
Athletic Transcentral football club is a football (soccer) club which plays in the
football league of Kyleland, a country in the continent of Wocia. The club is owned
by Athletic Transcentral Co, a company which has the sole objective of operating
the football club. For simplicity, the company and the club will be referred to as a
single entity, Athletic Transcentral (AT), in this exam.
You are an independent consultant advising AT on various matters. It is currently
March 20X4.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the case study:
1. New academy proposal summary: Summary by the football director about a proposal to
build a new academy
2. Stakeholders and reputation risk: Comments made at a board meeting discussing
stakeholder management and reputation risk
3. Marketing proposals: Summary by the marketing and IT director on enhancing the use of
social media and using data analytics to analyse social media activity
4. Enquiry into bribery allegations: Extract from a report by an independent investigator
examining allegations of bribery of players
Pre-seen information, which you have already used to familiarise yourself with
AT and the industry in which it operates, is also available on the left-hand side of
the screen.
This information should be used to answer each task within your chosen response
option(s).
Exhibits
Exhibit 1 - New academy proposal summary.
Exhibit 2 - Stakeholders and reputation risk.
Exhibit 3 - Marketing proposals.
Exhibit 4 - Enquiry into bribery allegations.
Pre-Seen Information
Attached as a separate file within the Marking Instructions QID
Task 1
(a) AT’s board is considering establishing its own academy. The football director
has prepared a proposal relating to the investment in the academy. The board of
directors has asked you to provide a balanced evaluation of the proposal. You
should assume that the funds for building the academy will be available without
any problems and you should not discuss this assumption.
Prepare a report for the board which advises on the financial and non-
financial benefits and drawbacks of the proposed establishment of the new
academy, and recommends with justification, whether the academy should
be established as proposed.
(16 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating evaluation skills by
assessing the factors which are most important in determining the
recommendation.
(4 marks)
(b) The directors also wish to be briefed on the key roles of the project sponsor
and project manager should they decide to go ahead with establishing an
academy.
Prepare briefing notes for the directors which advise on the roles and
responsibilities of the project sponsor and project manager, assuming the
building of the academy is undertaken.
(12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating commercial acumen skills
by showing insight into the roles and responsibilities of the project sponsor and
project manager in connection with the building of the academy.
(4 marks)
Task 2
(a) The board has recently been discussing how to manage stakeholders more
effectively. The Chair would like you to review the comments made by the three
directors at a recent board meeting.
Prepare an email to the Chair which discusses the views of the three
directors (other than the Chair) with regards to managing relationships with
different stakeholder groups.
(14 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating scepticism skills by
challenging courteously the views expressed by the three directors.
(4 marks)
(b) The marketing and IT director is concerned with AT’s use of social media in
communicating with stakeholders (particularly supporters). She is keen to use data
analytics to make the most of the information available from social media. The
marketing and IT director has prepared a summary of her proposals, but has had
to take urgent leave, so the chief executive officer wants you to provide the board
with a more detailed analysis of the benefits of the proposals.
Prepare briefing notes for the board which evaluate the benefits of:
(i)   enhancing the use of social media in AT’s e-marketing and communication with
stakeholders; and
(ii)   investing in big data analytics to analyse the data obtained from social media.
(14 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating analysis skills by
considering the benefits of the proposals to AT’s situation.
(4 marks)
(c) The Chair is particularly concerned about risks associated with stakeholder
management. These are reputation risk (because of the impact of a poor
reputation on relations with stakeholders) and data protection risk (because AT is
trying to obtain and hold more information about its supporters and customers). He
wants your assistance in briefing board members at the next board meeting of the
impact of these risks.
Prepare briefing notes for the board which assess the impacts of, and
recommend actions to address, the following risks:
(i)   reputation; and
(ii)   data protection.
(12 marks)
Task 3
A report has just been received from the independent investigator hired to look into
the allegations of bribery.
The chief executive officer (CEO) is concerned about the impact of the allegations
and that other players may be implicated. He wants to do a presentation to the
players explaining why bribery must be prevented, and also what AT and its
players need to do to combat bribery. He has asked you for help with the
presentation:
Prepare TWO slides and supporting notes for the CEO to present to the
players which:
(i)   explain why it is important to prevent bribery at AT; and
(ii)   advise them of the actions which AT and its players will need to take to
combat bribery.
(12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating communication skills by
clearly informing players of the importance of preventing bribery and of the actions
which the players need to take if they become aware of signs of bribery.
(4 marks)
Suggested Solution:
1(a)
Report to board of Athletic Transcentral (AT)
Evaluation of academy proposal
Introduction
This report evaluates the proposal to build a new academy.
Financial benefits
Income from player development
In the longer-term, developing players can improve AT’s financial position. Having a
bigger nucleus of players available from within AT will mean that fewer players have
to be bought in from outside, saving transfer fees and possibly the high wages
necessary to attract players from other clubs. Players can also be sold to other
clubs, generating transfer fee income.
Other income
The facilities are a potential source of income. They could be used by other clubs
and sports, generating cash flow during football’s close season. The lecture facilities
can also be used for conferences.
Naming
The academy provides an opportunity for sale of naming rights. Sponsoring the
facilities may appeal to commercial sponsors who will be able to access the facilities
and also be associated with the development and education of young players. Any
such arrangement is likely to be for the longer-term.
Saving hiring costs
Hiring other facilities is an expense which does not benefit AT. AT also does not
have control over the facilities of others which it uses.
Financial drawbacks
Difficulty of analysis
There is no estimation of the financial benefits of developing the academy. Ideally AT
should make a comparison between the significant expense of establishing and
running the academy, and the financial benefits.
However, any financial benefits from developing players are likely to be for the
longer-term and very difficult to estimate. An estimate could be made of future
transfer fees based on how other clubs have gained income from the sale of players
in their academies. However, this assumes the same success rate of developing
players at AT’s academy and estimates of future transfer fees, which are uncertain. It
is more difficult to estimate the impact of not buying players in, as that presupposes
knowledge of future playing staff strategies, as well as future transfer fees. This
makes conventional investment appraisal analysis very difficult.
Even if a return figure could be estimated, there is also the issue of what a
satisfactory return on the investment would be. Again, this would have to be
considered over the longer-term, but over what time-period is difficult to determine.
Other sources of income
The facilities’ generation of other income may also be doubtful. AT faces
considerable competition in obtaining conference and hospitality income. Any
commercial activities would have to be fitted round AT’s activities, or AT may need to
adjust its activities if facilities were hired out. The capacity of the training stadium
may be too small for other events or sports.
Alternative use of funds
The academy is likely to benefit AT in the longer-term. However, currently AT is
having trouble competing with other clubs, partly because of its lower stadium
capacity generating lower receipts. A more urgent use for funds would appear to be
for either a new stadium or to increase the capacity of the current stadium. There
may also be a need for more expenditure on the current stadium to fulfil stricter
health and safety requirements.
Non-financial benefits
Attraction of more players
The facilities at the academy and a more comprehensive development programme
will make it more likely that AT will produce high quality players. This should attract
more players from a wider community than currently. It will help AT compete better
with other clubs in signing young players. The emphasis on player safeguarding and
welfare, and all-round education, should help convince young players’ parents that
their children should join the academy.
Mission and corporate social responsibility
The stadium can be used to enhance links to the local community by providing the
facilities free of charge. This is in line with AT’s commitment in its values to the
community and publicising it can enhance AT’s reputation. Use by local schools and
children’s teams also creates links and enhances the chances that promising players
will join AT’s’ academy.
Forward planning
Having a regular stream of young players coming through from the academy into the
first team should enable longer-term planning. AT’s management should be able to
anticipate how younger players will be able to fill the gaps as older players retire or
leave AT when their contracts expire. This should reduce the need for AT to have to
enter the transfer market when there are gaps, helping stability.
Educating players
Developing players in the academy enables AT to promote its way of doing things.
This does not just apply to the way AT plays football but wider values, including
integrity and commitment. This should hopefully reduce the risk of ethical threats,
such as bribery. Having many first-team players who have come through the
academy is likely to improve AT’s overall culture.
Enhanced relationship with players
As players who have come through the academy have been at AT since they were
children, they may be more loyal to AT. There is likely to be a stronger relationship
between the players as they have known each other longer and understand how to
play together, resulting in better teamwork. As the players have been developed by
AT, team management should have a good understanding of these players’
strengths and weaknesses.
Non-financial drawbacks
Location
The academy would be completely separate from the main club and located some
distance away. There will inevitably be less contact between players in the first team
and the academy, and it may make it more difficult to integrate the academy’s
players into the rest of AT. If the location is out-of-town, it may be hard to hire out the
facilities.
Selling players to other clubs
If the academy predominately produces players who are sold to other clubs, AT may
gain the reputation for investing in player development but then realising their value
rather than using them to enhance its first team. Although AT may obtain significant
income from transfer fees, it may indicate to supporters and commercial sponsors
that AT lacks ambition. It may suggest AT prefers to receive funds from sales rather
than utilise the players developed to improve competitiveness.
Recommendation
Whilst the academy development will benefit AT in the longer-term, it seems that
expenditure on the current or a new stadium may be a more urgent priority. If
investment is made in the academy now, AT may not have the funds to enhance
capacity and may fall further behind other clubs. The idea of the more focused
approach to development that the academy would offer is a good one, but AT should
consider if an academy can be developed more cheaply than investing in the
purpose-built site.
Conclusion
This report has recommended that AT should consider if an academy can be
developed more cheaply than investing in the purpose-built site
1(b) Briefing notes for the board on roles and responsibilities of project
sponsor and project manager
Roles and responsibilities of project sponsor
Oversight roleThe project sponsor will be responsible for overseeing the overall
direction and objectives of the project. The sponsor will help develop the business
case and determine the critical success factors and key performance indicators,
defining the key benefits to AT that the academy will achieve.
Liaising with stakeholders
The project sponsor will be the main point of contact for key stakeholders and
responsible for liaising with them about the benefits which they can expect from the
academy. Key stakeholders will include the members of AT’s coaching staff
responsible for young players who would provide input about the facilities required.
The local community will also be important stakeholders. The project sponsor will
liaise with them about the impact of the building work and also what the new facilities
will provide for the community.
Reporting to board
The project sponsor will also discuss the benefits of the academy with the board and
ensure that the project has sufficient funding. It may be difficult to determine the level
of funding required, so it will be important for the project sponsor to ensure the board
understands the standard of facilities which the academy needs to have.
Liaising with project manager
The project sponsor will also oversee the control process which the project manager
will be responsible for implementing. This means ensuring that there are proper
controls in place including plans and timetables, budgets, specification of roles in the
project team and a clear project structure.
The project sponsor will also be involved in project planning. The sponsor needs to
give direction to the project team on the scope of the project, the level and standard
of the facilities to be provided. The project sponsor should also approve any major
changes in the project if they are required, if financial or logistical issues mean that
the scope of the facilities offered has to be scaled back.
The project sponsor is responsible for liaising with and guiding the project manager,
using the expertise which the project sponsor has. The project sponsor should
regularly receive reports and feedback from the project manager. The project
manager may also face difficulties during the delivery of the project, for example
disputes with contractors about project progress, which need to be escalated. The
project sponsor should help resolve these problems.
Roles and responsibilities of project manager
Day to day supervision of the projectThe project manager is responsible for
managing the project team on a day-to-day basis. The project manager is ultimately
responsible for delivering the project in the three years agreed for construction,
within the scope and costs agreed. At the end of the project, the project manager will
report on whether the project has been delivered within these constraints.
Project planning
The project manager will have detailed involvement in project planning, including
producing documentation such as the project initiation document and supporting
documentation such as budgets and network analysis. As part of planning, the
project manager should identify the financial and human resources required, and
with the assistance of the project sponsor, make sure that these are made available
at appropriate stages of the construction process.
A further responsibility of the planning process is identifying the main risks which
may impede the successful completion of the project and preparing contingency
plans to deal with them. These may include the impact of adverse weather,
particularly on the stadium where construction may be more complex.
Monitor project
The project manager should continually monitor how the project is proceeding. The
manager will be responsible for identifying and addressing any slippage against the
project plan. This may require discussing with the relevant outside contractors how
they can get back on schedule, particularly for the stadium where construction is
likely to involve a number of different stages, with each stage only starting when the
previous one has finished. Dealing with more serious slippages may require liaison
with the project sponsor with a view to changing the constraints governing the
project.
Managing the project team
The project manager is also responsible for managing those working on the project.
There is likely to be an internal project team, seconded from different parts of AT,
including under-18 team coaching and finance. The project manager will be
responsible for ensuring that team members work well together and communicate
effectively, both what they are doing and also any concerns which they may have.
The project manager will need to motivate them and ensure they know what the
project aims to achieve in terms of new facilities. The project manager is also
responsible for liaising with outside contractors, ensuring that their work is properly
supervised, and they supply the reports required.
Reporting
The project manager is responsible for reporting the project’s progress to the project
sponsor and ultimately the board. The project manager should provide regular
updates and more detailed reporting at the end of every stage of the project,
including whether the plans for future stages may need to be amended based on
what has happened.
2(a)
To: Chair
From: Consultant
Date: 5 March 20X4
Subject: Stakeholder management
Thank you for giving me the chance to comment on the directors’ views. I shall have
particular regard to how they propose dealing with stakeholder claims which may
conflict, and whether AT should go beyond its obligations and the claims
stakeholders have on it in order to enhance relationships with them.
Proposed strategies
Non-executive director
The non-executive director’s suggestion is one of satisficing, acknowledging that
stakeholder conflicts will exist and it is likely to be impossible to satisfy every
stakeholder completely. This policy has the advantage of fairness, that once a
stakeholder’s claim is acknowledged as legitimate, they have rights in relation to AT
which need to be satisfied to some degree.
However, the director does not suggest how AT should determine what criteria
should be used if stakeholder interests come into conflict to determine which
stakeholder should be satisfied and which disappointed. The approach does not
consider the extent to which stakeholder requirements are in the best interests of AT,
or whether they are unreasonable. The views also take no account of the power and
interest which stakeholders have. In particular, it will be necessary to meet the
requirements of some particularly powerful stakeholders even if others are
disappointed.
Finance director
The finance director’s views essentially address the non-executive director’s views,
in adopting the strategic positioning approach and using the criteria of level of power
and interest to determine stakeholder policy. Keeping key players happy – those with
high power and interest – has the vital advantage of guaranteeing AT’s existence. It
will mean that AT focuses on what it needs to do, for example fulfil league
regulations and contractual obligations with commercial sponsors and broadcasters.
However, it is solely governed by the impact which stakeholders can have on AT.
The commitment to corporate social responsibility suggests that it needs to consider
stakeholders which it particularly impacts as well. The local community, for example,
may be affected by noise and traffic on match days but may not be able to do
anything about it. Neglecting the interests of these stakeholders could result in their
power increasing over time, as for example, the local community could combine
together and put pressure on local politicians.
Marketing and IT director
Taking account of the influence that stakeholders have shouldn’t undermine the
policy of improving relationships via the use of social media. It should set the policy
in context and help manage the stakeholders appropriately.
The marketing and IT director’s suggestion of communication is the normal tactic for
dealing with stakeholders with high interest but low power. It can make stakeholders
feel more involved with AT, enhancing its brand. It is also consistent with the
enhancement of use of social media which is currently being proposed and will
enhance AT’s reputation as social media will show publicly the efforts AT is making
to communicate. Regular two-way communication with supporters in particular
enables AT to pick up quickly any issues that could adversely affect the supporters’
relationship with AT and result in reputation risk. Regular communication with
players can also help identify discontent which may make them vulnerable to bribery
approaches.
However, part of the communication policy will need to involve the management of
expectations, as some concerns may not be able to be addressed – for example,
improvements to stadium facilities being postponed while investment is made in the
new academy.
Kind regards
Consultant
2(b)
Briefing notes for the board on e-marketing proposals
Benefits of enhanced social media use
Emotional connection
Social media can be used to promote an emotional connection with AT, which is a
very important element of promoting long-term supporter loyalty. Social media
encourages supporters to interact through "likes" and “comments”. On-line
communities enable supporters to share their experiences, creating content which
will promote interest in AT. These forums can enable two-way communication,
enabling AT to see which aspects of the supporters’ experience can be promoted
further and the problems supporters have had which need to be addressed.
Global demographic
Social media enables AT to connect with supporters around the world in possibly
huge numbers. As well as sending out some content and messages globally, social
media can enhance the effectiveness of worldwide communication by enabling AT to
connect with supporters in their own language and context.
Younger demographic
Using the latest social media platforms enables AT to develop relationships with a
younger demographic who use these channels. Developing connections with
supporters at a young age can help establish their support for AT for the rest of their
lives, which is vital for securing AT’s long-term position.
News agenda
Regularly publishing news stories and giving supporters exclusive content shows
that AT wishes to connect with them. Social media also helps AT control the news
agenda about itself. The media is very interested in what AT is doing and may
publish stories which are speculation or damaging to AT. AT publishing stories early
on social media enables it to put forward its own position and may influence
subsequent media activity.
E-marketing
Social media can be an economic and flexible method of communication and
marketing. It can promote AT in multi-media ways, through images and videos as
well as text. AT can make use of viral marketing, for example to create interest in
future matches. Promotion on social media can also help direct supporters towards
digital media facilities through which AT can gain revenue, for example if AT starts
operating its own television channel. Social media can also link to product e-
commerce activities, for example promoting a new version of AT’s kit when it
becomes available.
Benefits of investing in data analytics
Wide coverage
Extending data analytics to social medial accounts used by other clubs and sports
can provide additional insights. By analysing how other clubs use social media, AT
can develop its own use of social media further. It can compare reactions to aspects
of its own operations, such as matchday experience, to reactions to these aspects at
other clubs in order to benchmark what it provides.
Patterns
Analysis of social media activity can provide patterns in data in relation to customer
requirements, as regards matchday experience and the products it sells.  This can
be used to determine what AT should do, if, for example, there are demands for
facilities to be more family friendly. Good or poor reactions to products can help
determine future stocking decisions. Seeing patterns may also help predict future
behaviour.
Users’ opinions
Social media analytics can provide wide-ranging evidence of users’ views. It can
analyse simple likes/dislikes or search out key words or images indicating a good or
bad reaction. It can also track opinions of all aspects of what AT is doing, including
team performance and supporter experience. This knowledge can help determine
what aspects users dislike which AT needs to address, and also how AT can
promote what it is doing on social media.
Content appeal
Social media analytics can look at specific aspects of what AT is doing on the social
media platforms which it uses. It can provide metrics such as the number of video
views. Popular content can remain on the site, and similar content can be posted
and less popular content removed.
Engagement
Social media analytics can measure the number of users and the overall level of
interest of users. It can track their level of engagement, including when they are
engaging, how much interaction there is on social media and how much content
users are posting themselves. This can help AT ascertain the appeal of the different
platforms it is using. Social media analytics can also measure the extent to which
content is disseminated to other users by, for example, retweets. This measures an
aspect of the level of marketing and brand awareness of AT. Analytics can also help
measure the most active users of social media in relation to AT and try to develop
relations with them.
Generation of income
Analysis of AT’s posts should help determine which posts are more successful in
driving sales of match tickets or of merchandise. AT can therefore make similar
posts in future.
2(c)
Briefing notes for board on reputation and data security risks
Reputation risk
Impacts
The impact of reputation risk will depend on the risk which has resulted in the threat
to reputation and also the reaction of stakeholders to it. It may be specific, for
example if AT has data breaches publicised, supporters and customers will be
unwilling to trust AT with their confidential information.
More generally, AT’s appeal depends on having a strong positive brand. Any
adverse impact on reputation could jeopardise the brand and hence revenue
streams. It may stop AT from expanding its supporter base and may also jeopardise
sales of merchandise.
Threats to reputation may also impair relations with commercial sponsors and result
in a loss of revenue from them. Commercial sponsors may not wish to renew their
sponsorship or may have termination clauses in their contracts which they can
exercise if association with AT threatens their own reputation. This could result in a
significant loss of short-term cash flow, impacting adversely on liquidity.
As a leading football club, AT receives considerable media coverage. Any event
damaging to reputation is likely to be well-publicised, exacerbating the problem.
Actions
As reputation risk is dependent upon other risks, it is important that AT identifies
those other risks which could pose a serious threat to reputation. The risk register
should highlight the consequences to reputation of those other risks materialising.
AT also needs to implement a contingency plan for other risks materialising and
reputation being threatened. This needs to include a quick public relations response
to try to minimise immediate damage. It should also include contacting and
reassuring important stakeholders, such as commercial sponsors who will be
particularly concerned about threats to reputation, that AT is taking effective action.
If reputation is damaged, AT needs to consider how to recover it. It may include
taking strong action to remedy the situation, for example dismissing any player who
has been found to be taking bribes. It may also mean showing how improved
controls and procedures should prevent the risk materialising in future.
Data protection risks
Impacts
Breaches of cybersecurity could lead to loss of data. It could also lead to
unauthorised data access, both of AT’s own data and data about its customers and
supporters.
If AT’s data protection precautions are inadequate, it could suffer legal action and
fines under data protection legislation. It could also be sued by supporters and
customers whose data has been accessed.
If AT’s failure to protect data is publicised, customers and supporters will not be
willing to trust it with their personal data. This will jeopardise AT’s initiatives to
develop closer relations with supporters. If customers are unwilling to do business
with AT on-line, this may threaten income streams. Any breaches may well be widely
publicised, given the media attention on AT, and this may threaten its reputation and
hence harm its brand.
Actions
AT needs to review its IT infrastructure to see if responsibility for data protection
controls is clear and has been communicated. The marketing and IT director needs
to take ultimate charge of it but be supported by personnel with specific security
responsibilities. AT needs to ensure that its procedures and documentation are up-
to-date and in line with data protection regulations, that staff have been adequately
trained in security procedures and the requirements of data protection regulations.
The external IT environment needs to be monitored and systems updated to counter
new threats.
Specific controls protecting data include encrypting sensitive data such as
supporters’ or customers’ personal details. Access to sensitive data should also be
restricted by passwords and two-factor authentication to as limited a number of staff
as possible. Controls over recruitment should ensure that appropriate references are
obtained for staff who have roles in relation to sensitive data.
Contingency arrangements should also be in place to ensure that data is properly
backed up and secured. A plan is needed for situations where breaches have
occurred, aiming to protect data as far as possible and contact those affected.
Slide 1
Importance of prevention of bribery
• Integrity of competition
• Our results
• Trust within club
• Legal penalties
• Reputation loss
• Stakeholders
The most important reason why bribery must be prevented is that bribery destroys
the integrity and fairness upon which football is based. Football is founded upon the
idea of players trying their best for their team. Bribery destroys their loyalty. Bribery
is also contrary to AT’s stated expectations that players will conduct themselves with
integrity.
Another obvious impact is that bribery could affect our results. It might only require a
single player to accept a bribe to lose and for that player to take action which gives
the game to the opposition. If bribery affects a number of games, the team will
achieve a lower number of points and may be threatened with relegation. Prize
money income will be reduced and also matchday income if the team is losing and
attendance is lower.
Bribery destroys the basis of trust within our club. If there are suspicions of bribery,
players may start questioning whether other players have made honest mistakes or
have deliberately played poorly because they have been bribed. These suspicions
can help destroy team spirit. Players may ultimately want to leave AT if they suspect
others are taking bribes.
If bribery is discovered, both players and clubs may face legal action. Players caught
taking bribes may be sent to prison. They may be banned for life from football.
Our club may also face legal penalties if players have taken bribes, or it has taken
insufficient action to prevent and detect bribery. AT may be fined or have points
deducted.
Bribery, if discovered and made public in the media, poses a serious reputation
threat to our club and other clubs in the league. Our club’s brand is underpinned by
the values we emphasise. Employing players who have taken bribes will therefore
destroy the value of the brand. Bribery also brings the reputation of the sport as a
whole into disrepute as it is seen as corrupt.
Bribery is therefore also likely to destroy the confidence of stakeholders. Supporters
will not want to support a club where players have been taking bribes. They may
stop watching football if they do not believe that the competition is fair. Commercial
sponsors may not want to be associated with a club or sport where bribery has
occurred. This could have a serious effect on sources of income (match tickets,
merchandise) for our club and for the league generally.
Slide 2
Actions
Club
• Training
• Code of conduct
• Whistleblowing channels
• Co-operation with league
Players
• Reporting of suspicions
• Whistleblowing other players
• Co-operation with investigation
Club
This session marks the start of training about bribery. Future sessions will cover in
detail the possible ways you may be approached and encouraged to take a bribe.
There will be regular updates as information becomes available about attempts at
bribery in football and other sports.
A code of conduct will be issued by AT stressing the need to maintain integrity and
the commitment which AT will demand of you. Compliance with the code of conduct
will be built into players’ contracts. The code will include a zero-tolerance policy in
relation to bribery, that any player suspected of taking bribes will be suspended. If
the allegations are proved, the player will be dismissed by AT and AT will support
legal action against them.
Whistleblowing channels will be made available with direct access for players and
other members of staff to report any suspicions of bribery. Anyone making a report
will be assured of confidentiality and that it will not affect their own careers adversely.
AT will also fully co-operate with any league initiatives to prevent or detect bribery.
This will include sharing of information about suspicions and approaches which have
been made to players.
Players
The code of conduct will give details of what you should do if you have suspicions
that bribery is being attempted. If you are approached yourself and you believe that
you are being asked to take a bribe, you should report this immediately to a director.
If you believe that other players may have been taking bribes, you should report your
suspicions in confidence through the whistleblowing channels which will be made
available. Don’t be worried that you aren’t certain, it is vital that AT’s management is
aware that bribery may be taking place and can investigate as soon as possible.
If investigation of bribery is undertaken at AT, you must fully and honestly state what
you know. Any failure to co-operate or withholding of information will result in serious
disciplinary action by AT.

stem_zh:
This case study relates to three tasks.
Corjetz is a low-cost regional airline business based in Corland.
You are a senior business analyst working in the finance department of Corjetz,
reporting directly to the chief finance officer. You are also often asked to carry
out tasks for other board members and senior managers.
It is currently September 20X3.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the case study:
1. Business magazine article – An article featuring an interview with the
chairman of Corjetz discussing the
leadership style adopted by Corjetz and the importance of demonstrating
corporate social responsibility.
2. Board meeting minutes extract – Extracts from the minutes of a recent
board meeting outlining two strategic
development proposals and the financing options available to support the
purchase of additional aircraft.
3. Email to the CEO – An email from the newly appointed chair of the Audit
Committee outlining the
risk management framework applied by Corjetz and several risks currently
being reviewed using this framework.
4. Teleconference notes – The teleconference notes from the chief
information officer outlining the applications
of data analytics and disruptive technologies.
The pre-seen information, which you have already used to familiarise
yourself with Corjetz and the industry in which it operates, is also available on
the left-hand side of the screen.
The above information should be used to answer each task within your
chosen response option(s).
Task 1 (28 marks)
Corjetz’s chairman has recently given an interview to a leading business
magazine about leadership at Corjetz.
The chief people officer (CPO) is about to lead an induction session for several
newly recruited staff, and she wants to include the chairman’s comments as
part of this session. She has asked for your help in preparing for this induction
session.
(a) Prepare TWO presentation slides and accompanying notes for the
induction session which:
(i) Explain the key qualities of entrepreneurship displayed by the
founders of Corjetz; and
(ii) Explain the impact which the board’s current leadership style has had
on shaping Corjetz’s culture.
(12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating communication skills
in clarifying and conveying relevant information about entrepreneurship and
the leadership style at Corjetz.
(4 marks)
Within his interview with the business magazine, the chairman also discussed
his view on the importance of showing leadership in corporate social
responsibility. The CPO has asked you to prepare notes on this which she will
use during the induction session.
(b) Prepare briefing notes for the CPO, which discuss the importance to
Corjetz of demonstrating corporate social responsibility to its
stakeholders.
(12 marks)
Task 2 (40 marks)
The CEO has asked for your assistance on two issues.
Firstly, two strategic development proposals were outlined along with possible
financing methods at a recent board meeting. The CEO wishes to examine
these proposals in more detail before the board decides which proposal to
select and has asked for your help.
(a) Prepare a report for the CEO which:
(i) Evaluates the two strategic development proposals, recommending
the most appropriate proposal for Corjetz; and
(14 marks)
(ii) Evaluates the sources of finance which are available, recommending
the most appropriate source of finance for Corjetz.
(6 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating scepticism skills in
challenging objectively the two strategic development proposals made by the
directors.
(4 marks)
Secondly, the CEO has just forwarded you an email which he has received
from the newly appointed chair of the Audit Committee, who has asked for
advice on the latest risk assessment report forwarded by Corjetz’s risk
manager.
(b) Prepare an email response to the chair of the Audit Committee which
assesses how Corjetz’s risk assessment framework could be applied to
each individual risk included in the latest risk assessment report.
(12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating analysis skills in
considering how Corjetz could effectively use the risk management framework
to assess the risks included in the recent risk assessment report.
(4 marks)
Task 3 (32 marks)
The CEO is due to host a teleconference with several of Corjetz’s key
shareholders. The aim of the teleconference is, firstly, to update the
shareholders on how Corjetz is applying data analytics to drive the business
forward and, secondly, to consider the disruptive technologies impacting on
the airline industry. The CEO has given you notes which were prepared by the
chief information officer (CIO) and has asked you to prepare a briefing paper
which can be presented to the key shareholders at the teleconference.
Prepare a briefing paper to be presented to the key shareholders at the
teleconference which:
(a) Discusses the benefits and challenges for Corjetz of using data
analytics in supporting its low-cost strategic position; and
(12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating evaluation skills in
objectively appraising the opportunities and challenges of data analytics in
supporting Corjetz’s low-cost strategic position.
(4 marks)
(b) Examines the potential impact of the disruptive technologies outlined
by the CIO on supporting Corjetz’s low-cost strategic position.
(12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating commercial
acumen skills in showing commercial judgement in considering the impact of
disruptive technologies on Corjetz.
(4 marks)

要求物有所值。70%的客户会货比三家，在最好的地点
购买最好的价格，这表明客户是成熟的，这便驱使HiLite
对他们的需求做出回应。
因此，HiLite应专注于客户的需求，尤其注重物有所值。
而其他如Waterbay Leisure和eeehols Group，这两家
Deeland历史最悠久的豪华连锁酒店都在倒闭了，很明
显，是因为他们没有有效地回应家庭客户的需求。
Related and supporting industries
The success of an industry can also be influenced by other industries/organisations which support it and supply it. In the
case of HiLite, our location in Deeland provides us with an advantage due to the highl y developed hotel and tourism
industries within the country. Travel companies both online and on the high street are thriving in Deeland, which clearly wil l
support and enhance the business opportunities of HiLite.
Online hotel booking websites and price comparison sites are also a key factor in driving the success of hotel businesses.
In particular, the price comparison websites, comparing our prices with our closest competitors drives our business to be
more competitive and to offer better and more flexib le pricing options for customers. Only those hotels which can respond
to this and are adaptable and flexible to customer pricing demands will survive and remain competitive. This is a potential
reason why the two luxury hotel chains mentioned in the previo us section have gone out of business this year.
In addition, growth in the business activity in the overall economy of Deeland, will also support the development of more
business travel in the country which is another factor in supporting the growth and d evelopment of our business traveller
provision and opportunities in Deeland.
Firm strategy, structure and rivalry
Success can be influenced by the way an organisation is created, organised  and managed. Evidence suggests, from the
comments made in the recent Chief Executive’s Report on Performance for 20X7/20X8 that in Deeland there is continuing
growth of the budget hotel market, as the luxury and mid -price sectors continue to decline. This is an obvious advantage
for HiLite, as we are the leading budget brand hotel chain in Deeland. Therefore, we are perfectly placed to exploit the
overall market conditions in the economy. Our brand is a key strength and we must ensure we maintain this stro ng brand
image and customer loyalty to the brand.
Our brand itself is the focus of our overall strategy, which is one of value for money and overall operating efficiency in or der
to ensure the best price for our customers. As stated above, we have highly  demanding business and leisure customers in
Deeland who are looking for value for money and best price and are prepared to shop around for this. Therefore, our
strategy of a value for money approach, as highlighted in the Chief Executive’s Report on Perfo rmance 20X7/20X8, should
assist in building upon this demand from customers and provide us with sustainable competitive advantage.
Competitive rivalry is also a key consideration, with growth in business activity in the economy driving more opportunities
and creating a highly competitive environment. As can be seen in the Hotel Industry Report for 20X8, two new national
branded chains of hotels have opened during this year in the main tourist centres of Deeland and national competition
remains intense. This rivalry is a key factor in encouraging us to innovate and be flexible to our customer demands, in
particular to respond to key trends in the industry, such as technological developments and in particular, the threat of
disruptive technologies. The two luxury chains of hotels which have recently gone out of business in Deeland may indeed
point to the fact that the intensity of rivalry in the industry is affecting the luxury end of the market more adversely than  the
more flexible budget hotels. This supports our overall strategic position as a budget hotel operator.
Government support
It is clear that the Deeland government is supportive of the travel and hotel industry in a number of ways. As already
mentioned, investment in infrastructure at both a regional and national level is a major influence on attracting travellers to
Deeland, in particular the investment in the new airport, as previously highlighted. Additionally, support in the education

经济型酒店更大。
sector to develop skilled and well-trained staff for the travel and tourism industry is also a key factor in ensuring a high level
of supply of talented and well-educated staff for our hotels.
A further factor highlighted in the Hotel Industry Report 20X8 is that the government of Deeland has recently announ ced
investment and tax breaks for employers offering job creation schemes in a wide range of industries, including travel and
tourism. Again, this points to a high level of government support to encourage a thriving business economy, including the
ourism and hotel industry. HiLite could take advantage of such tax breaks.
(b) To: The Chief business analyst
From: Senior business analyst
Date: xx/12/20X8
An evaluation of the proposed acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel chain
Introduction
The following report presents an evaluation of the proposal to acquire the Comfi Stay hotel chain located in Veelandia. The
report will consider the financial viability of the proposal and also consider a range of other criteria upon which t o base the
final decision and will evaluate the implications of this proposal for HiLite.
Financial considerations
Table 1: Financial evaluation of the proposal (Note all figures have been converted to the $)
From the net present value calculation of the proposed acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel chain (Table 1), it would appear
to be financially viable, returning an NPV of nearly $10· 6 million. This equates to a return on the initial investment of 13·2%.
Discounted payback occurs at approximately five years and three months.
Commented [ML77]: 政府的支持

因素，特别是新机场的投资。
此外，教育部门支持为旅游业培养技术娴熟、训练有素
的员工，这也是确保大量的酒店人才供给。
政府最近宣布了对雇主的投资和税收优惠，为包括旅游
和旅游业在内的广泛行业提供创造就业机会的计划。这
也表明，政府对鼓励蓬勃发展的商业经济给予了高度支
持，包括旅游业和酒店业。而 HiLite 可以充分利用这种
税收优惠。

据的一系列其他标准，并将评估该提案对希利特的影响。

折现回收期大约为5年零3个月。
However, it would be necessary to assess the viability and reliability of the data contained in the above NPV to assess how
realistic the revenue predictions are, based upon predict ed customer numbers and revenue per room calculations.
Economic conditions may be difficult to predict in a country such as Veelandia and we would need to take a cautious
approach in any forecast revenue results. Likewise, although our initial investment a nd refurbishment costs may be
relatively straightforward to assess, long-term costs in training and operations need to be carefully considered, particularly
in the light of the lack of investment which has been carried out in these hotels previously.
We should also consider foreign currency risk exposure, in relation to the stability of the Veelandia currency. We have
never operated any hotels overseas and this brings with it a number of challenges for our business in terms of translation
and transaction risks. We have carried out our NPV calculation in our own currency ($), but we must consider the potential
impact of the Veelandia currency fluctuations in particular on our forecasted revenues and operating costs.
Before making any decision based upon these financial results, I would like to see possibly a range of NPV calculations
based upon best and worst-case scenarios. In addition, before proceeding with the acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel
chain, we should also consider a range of other iss ues which are set out below:
Strategic fit of the proposed acquisition
We should assess whether the proposed acquisition of Comfi  Stay is in line with our strategic position; that is, does it have
a strategic fit with the rest of our business? We should also consider the cultural fit with our existing operations, particu larly
as the Comfi Stay hotel chain is located in a different c ountry, 5,000 miles away.
As the Comfi Stay hotel chain is within our current sphere of business operations as a hotel, it would be considered to have
a strategic fit. However, the strength of that strategic fit may be questionable, based on the fact tha t it is a mid-range hotel
chain offering additional facilities such as gyms and pool facilities. Our current HiLite hotels located in Deeland do not of fer
such additional facilities and we would need to carefully consider how to integrate Comfi Stay’s hote ls into the HiLite brand,
which is based upon a ‘budget’ offering. Therefore, strategic fit may not be as clear -cut as we may think.
However, it is evident from the recent Hotel Industry Report for 20X8, that consolidation in the marketplace is becoming
common, and hotels operating within different sectors of the market are consolidating through acquisition. Therefore, it
would seem that this option could be suitable, based upon recent acquisition activity in the market. Also, our chief
executive has suggested in his Report on Performance for 20X7/20X8 that HiLite is considering the strategic direction of
developing the HiLite brand overseas and therefore this would exploit this potential opportunity for us.
Another consideration is the cultural fit of the Comfi Stay hotel chain. As Veelandia is located over 5,000 miles away from
Deeland and the fact that the hotels are not budget hotels, there may be cultural differences to consider. The fact that it i s
a family-run business may also mean that it has a family-based culture which may be difficult to change and integrate into
our current operations and style of management.
National culture is also clearly different in Veelandia and may be challenging for HiLite. Poor employment practices and
evidence of use of under-age labour in the hotel industry in Veelandia must be thoroughly investigated and we must make
sure that our own cultural behaviours are transferred to replace ones which are clearly not suitable for our own operations.
This must be a priority for HiLite, as our own reputation could be damaged by association with the Comfi Stay hotel chain if
Commented [ML82]: 对于财务数据的的深入思考：

Veelandia这样的国家，经济状况可能很难预测，所以需
要对任何收入预测结果采取谨慎的态度。并且，尽管初
始投资和翻新成本可能相对容易评估，但在培训和运营
方面的长期成本需要仔细考虑。

Acceptability of the acquisition
When considering the acceptability of the proposed acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel chain, we need to evaluate whether
it is consistent with our current objectives in terms of risk and return, and importantly whether it will be acceptable to our
stakeholders.
When considering the calculations of net present value, it would be prudent to test the sensitivity of some of our
assumptions to evaluate the overall impact of and potential sensitivity of these factors on the final outcome. For example, a
failure to achieve the expected occupancy levels from year 3 onwards could reduce the final NPV significantly, as expected
revenues would be significantly reduced. Any combination of variable changes needs to be tested, to assess our sensitivity
to potential changes in the underlying assumptions made and the risks which these changes may pose. We must also
verify the accuracy of our assumptions.
We also need to consider acceptability of the proposed acquisition to our stakeholders. Our own staff based in Deeland
may be largely indifferent to the decision, but those hotel managers who may be requested to re -locate may potentially be
reluctant (or conversely may indeed be enthusiastic at the proposal). We must ensure that this is communicated effectively
to these hotel managers in good time, should the decision to acquire Comfi Stay be made. Staff in Veelandia will likely
react positively to this investment, if it means that employment will continue and there is likely to be a high possibility o f
improved employment rights and an improvement in working conditions and opportunities for training.
Our shareholders are likely to be favourable towards the strategy if it creates a positive return on shareholder wealth. The
chief executive has clearly stated in his Report on Perf ormance that this is a proposed strategy and therefore shareholders
will be accepting of this business development opportunity, as long as it impacts favourably on the profits of the business.
However, we must be mindful of the impact of this decision on o ur shareholders if they believe this decision may impact
adversely on our reputation, as this could indeed impact adversely on shareholder wealth. Effective and regular
communication with our shareholders must be carried out.
Customers in Veelandia may not favour this direction for Comfi Stay hotels if it means an increase in prices. However,
hotel refurbishments and updates should increase the quality of the hotel offering, which have clearly become outdated
due to lack of investment. This improvement to the quality of the Comfi Stay hotels should satisfy potential customers. We
also need to consider how the wider community and the media will react to this decision. There is a risk that it will be seen
as unfavourable by the wider public, as Veelandia doe s not operate to the standards of Deeland and may be seen as being
unethical to operate in a low wage economy. We must make it clear to these stakeholders that it is our intention to operate
at the highest ethical standards within Veelandia, should the acq uisition be successful. For example, we should follow strict
guidelines on offering a fair wage to employees and not allowing under -age workers.
Assessment of resource requirement of the proposed acquisition
We must also consider whether we have the resources we need to carry out the acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel chain.

equity. We are a highly profitable business and shareholders will be expecting us to continue the growth in profit which we

(a) PRESS RELEASE
For immediate release
HiLite’s proposed acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel chain, Veelandia
HiLite Hotels, Deeland’s most popular and largest budget hotel chain, has recently announced its intention to acquire the
family-run ‘Comfi Stay’ chain of hotels, located in Veelandia. Since the announcement, there have been some concerns
raised that the acquisition of the Comfi Stay hotel chain goes against the high principles and standards which HiLite strives
to attain.
HiLite wishes to make clear the following points in response to these concerns:
1. Employees’ rights
Although the Comfi Stay hotel chain is located in a low wage economy, it would be HiLite’s intention to ensure that its staff
located in Veelandia are not exploited in any way, and if it is found that child labour is being used or staff are working
excessive hours, then this practice will immediately be ceased on acquisition. HiLite values its hotel staff highly and is
committed to ensuring that it is a great place to work, and this commitment will also be given to its hotel staff in Veelandia.
It is not unethical for HiLite to pay staff in Veelandia below the rates which its staff in Deeland are paid, particularly if the
wage rates are favourable when benchmarked within the local economy, for similar employees in the hotel sector. This is
common business practice across many industries  operating in multiple locations. However, as stated above, it is not the
intention of HiLite to exploit staff located in Veelandia. HiLite invests around $10 million annually in skills and developme nt
programmes for its staff in Deeland, and has implement ed the new Deeland National Living Wage six months in advance
of the government’s launch date. Therefore, rates of pay will be reviewed for all staff and will be commensurate with the
role undertaken and the expected and fair wage rate in Veelandia. We wil l provide training opportunities for all staff and will
also offer employment contracts which will give employees security of employment.
2. Employment opportunities
HiLite’s intention is to provide employment and development opportunities for the staf f employed in all of its hotels. Over
the next six years, HiLite intends to invest $10 million in training and staff development in Veelandia to ensure staff there
are as highly skilled as those operating in Deeland.
In line with our diversity and equality policies, HiLite will immediately cease the policy of only employing male hotel
managers and will implement a policy of selection for senior roles based upon experience and suitability for the role and
not based upon gender, age or ethnicity. Obviously, HiLite will need to ensure cultural sensitivity in all of its employment
activities but will ensure that its own employment policies, in terms of equality and diversity, are adhered to, wherever it is
located throughout the world.
3. Environmental investment
In terms of the environmental impact of the decision to acquire the Comfi Stay hotel chain, HiLite intends to invest $6
million over the next six years in environmental investment in the Comfi Stay hotels. This  should go some way towards
prevention of further incidents of pollution which have recently occurred in some of its hotels.
In addition, a thorough investigation of environmental practices and policies of the whole business and at individual hotel

(b) For the attention of the Finance Director
Notes on the role and benefits of integrated reporting to stakeholders
Aims of integrated reporting in effective communication
If we are to implement an integrated reporting approach, we should do so with the aim to improve the quality of information
we provide to our stakeholders and to promote a more efficient approach to our corporate reporting activities which
consolidates and draws upon different communication strands and methodologies. This should enable us to present a
more efficient, cohesive approach to stakeholder communication.
Role of integrated reporting
HiLite’s integrated report should be designed to benefit all of our stakeholders who are interested in how we create value
over time, including our hotel staff, the hotel customers, local communities  where our hotels are located, suppliers and, of
course, our investors. Importantly, our ability to create value is linked directly to the value we create for those stakeholders.
The primary role of an integrated report is to improve the quality of infor mation available to our stakeholders, by
communicating broader and more relevant information which can assist them in effective decision making, in particular for
our investors (however, investors form only a part of this system). Research indicates that i nvestors want broader
information which goes beyond pure financial data, similar to the needs of our wider stakeholder groups mentioned above.
The benefits of integrated reporting

The benefits to stakeholders of using a broader information set are nume rous and have been highlighted in several studies
which have surveyed stakeholder attitudes to such information. The benefits include (amongst others): an improved
relationship between HiLite and our investors, a greater insight for our hotel customers and  our hotel staff into our
organisation’s business model and the benefits we can offer them, strategy and long -term outlook, as well as a greater
understanding of our use and dependence on different resources and ‘capitals’ and our access to and effect on t hem.
HiLite’s investors in particular would benefit from such information which informs investment decision -making. Using a
broader set of information can also benefit them by providing a greater understanding of the drivers of our performance
and value creation. With this information, they can be more confident of our long -term outlook and our ability to create
value over time.
Benefits recognised at the company level could also impact on our stakeholders. For example, studies suggest that
companies who adopt an integrated reporting approach outperform those which do not and have more long -term investors.
This will clearly benefit all of our stakeholders, including our hotel staff and our customers.
Building relationships with our stakeholders
By providing information which stakeholders need, value and find useful will help us build long -term relationships with
these stakeholders. Information which stakeholders would value includes an overall explanation of our business model,
how we generate our business and a well-articulated strategy and a clear understanding of our anticipated future
opportunities/direction and risks. Stakeholders could then use this information in many ways, including to help manage
investment risk, evaluate the hotel industry dynamics and the regulatory environment and to assess HiLite’s long -term
prospects and stability.
This will be useful for our investors, but would also be important to our staff who are looking for employment stability and
future employment prospects. Our local communities will also wish to see our commitment to the local economy in to the
future. As a hotel business, we can stimulate local economies by bringing in tourists to the area to spend money in the
local economies in which we operate.
Integrated reporting could help us to focus on aspects which materially affect our long -term ability to create value. Through
communication to our stakeholders and transparency, the application of integrated reporting allows us to ‘tell our own story’ ,
preventing analysts and external observers (such as the recent newspaper article) from making assumptions on our behalf.
Although institutional investors are largely interested in the financial performance and quantitative key performance
indicators of HiLite, assessing other performance indicators is also likely to play a pivotal role in an investment decision -
making process, including wider information for instance, environmental, social and governance issues, which will help
investors to assess investment risks more fully.
Reporting on broader areas of value creation is likely to be important to a wide range of stakeholders also, offering an
insight into business strategy, performance, governance and prospects, encouraging improved relationships w ith
customers, employees, suppliers and local communities. Stakeholders are able to use integrated reporting information in
the following ways:
– Economic analysis: to understand trends and externalities in the hotel industry which could affect the economi c outlook
and value creation of HiLite.

者(如最近的报纸文章)做出假设。
虽然机构投资者主要关注财务业绩和量化关键业绩指标，
但评估其他业绩指标也可能在投资决策过程中发挥关键
作用，包括更广泛的信息，例如环境、社会和治理问题，
这将有助于投资者更全面地评估投资风险。
– Industry analysis: to understand factors driving competitiveness in the hotel industry and the potential for sustained
value
creation in an industry.
– Company strategy: to understand management quality and corpora te strategy and evaluate our ability to respond to
emerging trends in the hotel industry, such as disruptive technologies and consolidation of the industry.

当地社区的关系。利益相关者可以通过以下方式使用综
合报告信息:

能力酒店行业的新兴趋势，如颠覆性技术和行业整合。
3.  At the most recent board meeting of HiLite, a discussion occurred on the potential threat of disruptive
technologies in the hotel industry. Following this meeting, the chief executive has requested further
information for the board on this issue.
Required:
(c) The chief business analyst has asked you to write a report for the next board
meeting which discusses the potential challenges to HiLite posed by the
development of the disruptive technologies which are emerging in the hotel industry,
and highlig ht the potential application of disruptive technologies which may be
considered   by HiLite. (14 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating scepticism skills in
probing and challenging the opinions of the board. (4 marks)
At the board meeting, the finance director presented information on three risks in HiLIte’s current
information systems environment and the board has requested further analysis of these risks.
Required:
(d) The finance director has asked you to prepare thr ee presentation slides (one for
each risk identified) and accompanying  notes for the next board meeting
assessing the potential outcomes of each of the information systems  risks
identified by the finance director and recommend actions for each risk which
HiLite should take in order to control these risks. (12 marks)
Professional skills marks are available for demonstrating commercial acumen skills
in demonstrating insight and understanding of the information system risks and
controls. (3 marks)
(33 marks)

就颠覆性技术对酒店业的潜在威胁进行了讨论。在这次

息。
